symptom: Azure VM enters reboot loop or goes to Stopped state after installing Windows cumulative updates (e.g., KB4556813). VM screenshot shows boot process getting interrupted and restarting repeatedly.
rootCause: When VM is shutdown from Azure portal, Windows Updates get applied during shutdown. Azure Host Agent has a 10-minute timeout to forcefully turn off the VM. If updates take longer than 10 minutes, Host Agent forcefully shuts down the VM, leaving it in non-boot state due to partially completed update process.
solution: Offline troubleshooting required: attach OS disk to repair VM, uninstall the problematic cumulative update(s). Collect WU data (DISM /Image logs, CBS logs) for analysis. Customer-facing: explain the 10-min timeout mechanism and recommend applying updates during maintenance windows.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/Non boot/Windows Fails to Boot After Cumulative Updates_RDP SSH

symptom: Azure VM screenshot shows Windows Update in progress ('Working on updates ##% complete') or reverting ('We couldn't complete the updates, Undoing changes') - VM stuck during update installation or rollback
rootCause: During KB installation or rollback, OS needs time to process updates. Large number of KBs installed at once increases chance of OS corruption. If interrupted, OS may enter servicing loop or become unreliable.
solution: Wait for update to complete (can take hours). If stuck in loop: offline troubleshooting - attach OS disk to repair VM, run DISM to uninstall problematic updates or fix component store corruption. If OS corruption is severe, rebuild may be needed. Ref: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/troubleshooting/troubleshoot-stuck-updating-boot-error
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/Non boot/Windows Update_RDP SSH

symptom: Windows VM screenshot shows Linux/Grub boot process instead of Windows boot - the VM is encrypted with CloudLink third-party encryption software
rootCause: CloudLink encryption uses a Linux-based machine to manage encryption keys. If the CloudLink vault fails to retrieve the client encryption key, the Windows VM shows interaction with the CloudLink Linux server and fails to boot.
solution: Engage CloudLink support to troubleshoot key retrieval. If Bitlocker team involvement needed, set up machine on Nested virtualization first, then cut a problem to Windows Onprem Bitlocker team.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/Non boot/Windows VM with a Linux Screenshot_RDP SSH

symptom: Windows 11 Trusted Launch VM fails to boot with error 'The boot loader did not load an operating system' after EFI partition was deleted and recreated
rootCause: When the EFI partition is deleted and recreated at a different location, the VM boot order (encoded in NVRAM) still points to the old location. Trusted Launch firmware does not fall back to default boot location when entries fail.
solution: Deallocate VM, download VHD, upload to new storage account, create new managed disk from VHD with Security Type=Trusted Launch and leave VM Guest State Blob blank (Azure creates new guest state blob), then swap OS disk.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/Non boot/Windows11 TPM won't boot in Azure The boot loader did not load an operating system_RDP SSH

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot with 'The boot loader did not load an operating system' after converting from Gen 1 to Gen 2 and rebuilding BCD
rootCause: Converting from Gen 1 to Gen 2 and rebuilding BCD can result in improper boot configuration, causing the VM to fail to boot.
solution: Deallocate VM, download VHD, create new managed disk with Trusted Launch security type and blank VM Guest State Blob, swap OS disk on the problematic VM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/Non boot/Windows11 TPM won't boot in Azure The boot loader did not load an operating system_RDP SSH

symptom: VM screenshot shows 'Working on features ##% complete Don't turn off your computer' and VM is stuck not booting
rootCause: Customer added or removed a Windows Server role or feature, and the operation is taking long or is stuck.
solution: Take multiple screenshots to validate if progress is being made. Only intercede if truly stuck to avoid breaking the role/feature. If stuck, use OFFLINE troubleshooting approach with rescue VM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/Non boot/Working on Features_RDP SSH

symptom: RHEL 7/8 Gen 2 Azure VM fails to boot with 'Failed to open \EFI\redhat\grubx64.efi Not Found' - reboot loop from Hypervisor attempting to locate EFI boot file
rootCause: Missing grubx64.efi file from the EFI system partition. Can be caused by misconfiguration during OS/grub2 installation, accidental deletion, or incomplete/interrupted software updates (yum/dnf).
solution: Create RHEL Gen2 rescue VM with same distro, attach broken OS disk copy, mount and enter chroot, run 'yum reinstall grub2-common grub2-pc grub2-efi-x64 grub2-pc-modules grub2-tools-minimal grub2-tools-extra grub2 grubby grub2-tools shim', then 'grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg', exit chroot, swap OS disk.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/Non boot/rhel non boot grubx64 efi not found_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP to Windows 2008 R2 VM returns 'Access is denied' during authentication, but administrative session (mstsc /admin) works. Remote Desktop Licensing service crashes with Event ID 7024.
rootCause: Lack of permissions for users to read the certificate registry entries on terminal services (HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\SystemCertificates\Remote Desktop\Certificates).
solution: Grant READ access to 'Remote Desktop Users' group on registry key HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\SystemCertificates\Remote Desktop\Certificates.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Access Is Denied Responding_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP to Windows VM returns 'Access is denied' during authentication due to user profile loading errors caused by conflicting group policies
rootCause: Login error while loading user profiles, caused by user policy conflicts on the profile.
solution: Set registry: Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Terminal Server' -name 'IgnoreRegUserConfigErrors' 1 -Type DWord -force, then restart VM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Access Is Denied Responding_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP to Windows VM returns 'Access is denied' when user belongs to many AD groups and nested AD groups - Kerberos token too small
rootCause: Kerberos token size not big enough to hold all permissions. Happens when user belongs to many AD groups and nested groups, especially on legacy systems with low default MaxTokenSize.
solution: Increase Kerberos MaxTokenSize: Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Kerberos\Parameters' -name 'MaxTokenSize' 65535 -Type DWord -force, then restart VM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Access Is Denied Responding_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP to Windows VM returns 'Access is denied' because Terminal Service (TermService) is starting with incorrect service account
rootCause: The Terminal Service is configured to start with an incorrect account instead of NT Authority\NetworkService.
solution: Fix TermService ObjectName: Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\services\termservice' -name 'ObjectName' 'NT Authority\NetworkService' -Type String -force, then restart VM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Access Is Denied Responding_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP to Windows Server 2016 Nano VM fails with 'machine is offline' error - screenshot shows Nano Server console without GUI
rootCause: Windows Server 2016 Nano is a stripped-down version with no GUI, no RDP support, and no local logon support by design.
solution: Use PowerShell remoting instead of RDP. Ensure NSG/ACL allows ports 5985 and 5986. Connect via Enter-PSSession -ComputerName <IP> -Credential (Get-Credential), or use WMI/WinRM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Access to Server 2016 Nano_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP via site-to-site VPN/VNET from on-premises shows black screen after authentication, disconnects after ~1 minute with 'connection lost' error. No Event 4005 in SYSTEM logs.
rootCause: MTU size is set incorrectly on the on-premises firewall, causing packets larger than the MTU to be dropped (DF flag set).
solution: Update MTU size on on-premises firewall. Or change locally on VM: netsh interface ipv4 set subinterface 'Local Area Connection' mtu=1420 store=persistent. Verify with: netsh int ipv4 show subint.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen Due to MTU Size_RDP SSH

symptom: Black screen after entering RDP credentials; user profile does not finish loading; VM has network connectivity and RDP is responsive but session stays on black screen
rootCause: Winlogon cannot complete logon and policy processing; a process or thread is deadlocked on a shared resource, preventing the session from progressing to the interactive desktop
solution: Collect a memory dump while the VM is in the failing state and analyze it to identify the blocking thread/process. Run 'dism /online /cleanup-image /restorehealth' to repair corrupted OS components. If DISM finds unrepairable corruption, collect dism.log and cbs.log for further analysis
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen Generic_RDP SSH

symptom: Black screen on both VM console screenshot and RDP session; Desktop Window Manager (dwm.exe) crashes with error 0xc000007b or Application Error Event ID 1000 (ntdll.dll fault); other connections (SMB, RPC, WinRM) work fine
rootCause: OS file corruption affecting the GUI subsystem, specifically dwm.exe; related to Azure VM data corruption issue documented in KB3137061
solution: Apply KB3137061 fix. For file corruption, run SFC /scannow and DISM /online /cleanup-image /restorehealth to repair. Check CBS logs for specific corrupted components. If unrepairable, rebuild VM from a known good image
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen on RDP and Console_RDP SSH

symptom: Black screen on RDP that disconnects after about 1 minute; VM shows high resource/performance usage; Resource Exhaustion Detector Event ID 2004 warns of low virtual memory
rootCause: VM is experiencing a performance spike or virtual memory exhaustion due to applications consuming excessive memory (e.g., MySQLWorkbench, Java processes)
solution: Identify resource-heavy processes via Task Manager or Get-Process. Reduce memory-intensive application footprint, add more VM memory by resizing, or restart the VM to temporarily resolve
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen on RDP but then Disconnects_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP shows black screen then disconnects; VM is under brute force RDP attack causing performance degradation; high CPU/memory usage observed in Host Analyzer
rootCause: Brute force RDP attack over the internet causing CPU/memory performance spike, exhausting resources needed for legitimate RDP sessions
solution: Enable Azure NSG rules to restrict RDP access to known IP ranges; enable JIT VM access; consider using Azure Bastion instead of public RDP exposure
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen on RDP but then Disconnects_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP shows black screen then disconnects; explorer.exe crashes with Application Error Event ID 1000 (faulting module TwinUI.dll, exception 0x80270233)
rootCause: Explorer.exe process crashes due to TwinUI.dll fault, preventing the desktop shell from loading during RDP session
solution: Run SFC /scannow and DISM /online /cleanup-image /restorehealth to repair the corrupted TwinUI.dll. If the issue persists, check for pending Windows Updates that may contain a fix for the specific OS build
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen on RDP but then Disconnects_RDP SSH

symptom: Black screen after RDP credentials on Windows Server 2012 R2; VM in partial hang state; apps (e.g., SQL) run slowly; reboot temporarily fixes the issue
rootCause: Deadlock in WinHttpAutoProxySvc: when this service is disabled and the system is under stress, RPCSS has multiple threads trying to get info from WinHttpAutoProxy, causing a system-wide deadlock
solution: Enable the WinHttpAutoProxySvc service (set to Manual or Automatic). Apply latest Windows Server 2012 R2 cumulative updates that include the fix for this deadlock condition
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen on RDP_RDP SSH

symptom: Black screen after RDP on Windows 10 RS3 VM deployed with single CPU (MicrosoftWindowsDesktop.Windows-10.RS3-Pro image); OS does not complete initialization
rootCause: Known issue with Windows 10 RS3 image: when deployed with only 1 CPU, the OS hangs during initialization while trying to start the Azure VM Agent service
solution: Resize the VM to a size with 2 or more CPUs. With 2 CPUs the OS will complete initialization (though slowly at first). More CPUs = faster first boot completion
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen on RDP_RDP SSH

symptom: Black screen after RDP on domain-joined VMs; boot diagnostic shows spinning circle; dwm.exe crashes with error 0xc0000142 (Application Popup in System event log); reproducible on any VM joined to same domain
rootCause: Desktop Window Manager (dwm.exe) fails to start with error 0xc0000142 on domain-joined VMs, likely due to Group Policy or domain configuration affecting the DWM initialization
solution: Check Group Policy applied to the VMs for settings affecting DWM or desktop composition. Test with a non-domain-joined VM to confirm domain-related root cause. Review Application Popup events for dwm.exe startup failures
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Black Screen on RDP_RDP SSH

symptom: After entering RDP credentials, Windows GUI does not load and only an administrator CMD window appears; CBS logs show ResolvePendingTransactions error with STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED
rootCause: .NET Framework was removed from the Windows Server system, typically through the IIS console feature uninstallation, which also removes the Windows Server GUI components that depend on .NET
solution: Reinstall .NET Framework using Server Manager or DISM. See 'Removing .NET Framework 4.5/4.5.1 removes Windows 2012/2012R2 UI and other features' blog for recovery steps. May need offline repair if GUI is completely broken
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/CMD Windows Upon RDP_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP error 'Because of a protocol error detected at the client (code 0x1104), this session will be disconnected'; error appears immediately without credential prompt; other connections (SMB, RPC) work
rootCause: Another application or service has bound to RDP port 3389 instead of Terminal Services (termservice.exe), causing RDP protocol negotiation failure
solution: Run 'netstat -anob' to identify the process listening on port 3389. Either stop the conflicting app and restart TermService, or change RDP port via registry key HKLM SYSTEM CurrentControlSet Control Terminal Server WinStations RDP-Tcp PortNumber, then restart TermService and update NSG/firewall
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Client Code 0x1104_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP connection hangs on 'Configuring remote session' when connecting to Azure VM; OS is fully loaded and waiting for credentials; admin session (mstsc /admin) may work
rootCause: The server is unable to reach the RD license server or the license server information is not configured on the machine
solution: Configure RD Licensing on the server: ONLINE via Serial Console using RD License setup EMS template, or OFFLINE by attaching OS disk to rescue VM and configuring RD License registry settings
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Configuring Remote Session_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP connection fails using MSTSC or MSRDC client older than version 1.2.675; errors include 'Something went wrong' connection/subscription block on Windows Desktop client
rootCause: All connections from old legacy RDP clients (MSTSC and MSRDC older than 1.2.675) are blocked because they are not secure enough. Applies to Windows 10, 8, 8.1 and 7
solution: Update to the latest Windows Desktop Remote Desktop client (MSRDC version 1.2.675 or later). Download: 64-bit https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?linkid=2068602, 32-bit https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?linkid=2098960, ARM64 https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?linkid=2098961
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Connect using MSTSC or MSRDC Fails_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP fails with 'The connection was denied because the user account is not authorized for remote login' or 'To sign in remotely, you need the right to sign in through Remote Desktop Services'
rootCause: The user trying to login via RDP is not a member of the local Remote Desktop Users group. By default only Administrators or Remote Desktop Users local groups can RDP
solution: Add the user to Remote Desktop Users group: run net localgroup remote desktop users username /add via Serial Console, Run Command, or CSE. For DCs, update Allow log on through RDS GPO. No restart required.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Connection Denied User Account not Authorized_RDP SSH

symptom: Cannot RDP to Azure VM with generic 'Remote Desktop cannot connect' three-reasons error; PSPING responds, SMB and Event viewer work, VM screenshot shows OS loaded
rootCause: Remote Desktop Services connection was disabled on the machine by the customer
solution: Re-enable Remote Desktop Services connection: ONLINE via Serial Console using TS-RDP-Enable EMS template, or OFFLINE by attaching OS disk to rescue VM and enabling RDP via registry
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Connection Disabled from RDSession Host_RDP SSH

symptom: Azure VM OS fully loaded but cannot connect; DNS Client (DNSCache) service not running; Event ID 7022 DNS Client service hung on starting, or Event ID 7001 dependency failure, or Event ID 7000 service failed to start
rootCause: DNS Client (DNSCache) service is not running due to: service disabled, crashing/hanging, dependency service (NSI/TDX) failure, logon failure, or service account mismatch in shared svchost container
solution: Diagnose via sc query DNSCACHE: if disabled set sc config DNSCACHE start=auto and start; if dependency failure fix NSI/TDX; if error 1058 persists restore DNS Client ACL with sc.exe sdset, check GPO, verify dnsrslvr.dll exists, remove stale ImagePath registry entry
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/DNS Client Service not Starting_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP fails with 'Because of an error in data encryption, this session will end. Please try connecting to the remote computer again'; SecurityLayer and UserAuthentication set to 0
rootCause: Incorrect SecurityLayer and UserAuthentication registry values; encryption level (MinEncryptionLevel) mismatch between server and client; possibly TLS protocols disabled or FIPS mode enabled
solution: 1) Temporarily disable NLA: set UserAuthentication=0 via Serial Console or Run Command. 2) Lower MinEncryptionLevel from 3 to 2 at HKLM SYSTEM CurrentControlSet Control Terminal Server WinStations RDP-Tcp. 3) Ensure TLS 1.0/1.1/1.2 enabled in SCHANNEL Protocols. 4) For domain-joined, temporarily disable gpsvc. Re-enable NLA after fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Data Encryption Error_RDP SSH

symptom: Azure IaaS VM reboots every hour; user can log in after reboot; Guest OS Event logs show Dynamics, IIS, SQL errors; Availability Explorer shows repeated CustomerInitiated Update cycles
rootCause: Expired Dynamics AX trial license causing the system to automatically reboot every hour
solution: Transfer the entire case to the Dynamics AX queue. Update case Product to Dynamics AX 2012 R3, Support Topic to Dynamics. Customer may need to re-arm Windows trial license.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Dynamics AX Reboot_RDP SSH

symptom: RDP fails with three-reasons error on VM with Citrix XenApp 6.x/7.x installed or recently uninstalled; standard RDP troubleshooting exhausted, ping and telnet port 3389 work fine
rootCause: Citrix XenApp 6.x/7.x RDS VDA modifies RDP listener LoadableProtocol_Object registry to RPM.CtxRdpProtocolManager; uninstalling XenApp sometimes fails to restore the original value
solution: Restore LoadableProtocol_Object registry to original GUID: for Server 2008 R2 use {18b726bb-6fe6-4fb9-9276-ed57ce7c7cb2}, for Server 2012+ use {5828227c-20cf-4408-b73f-73ab70b8849f}. Remove leftover ICA-tcp registry key. Ref: Citrix CTX136390
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Fail RDP Connection on Citrix VM_RDP SSH

symptom: Winlogon crashes after entering credentials when RDPing to Azure VM with Citrix installed; Event 4005 Windows logon process unexpectedly terminated; VM restarts normally but crashes on next login
rootCause: Citrix injects the binary TWI3.dll into the winlogon process; a faulty version of this DLL causes winlogon to crash
solution: Update or remove the faulty Citrix TWI3.dll. If Citrix is no longer needed, fully uninstall all Citrix components. Contact Citrix support for a patched version.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Fail RDP Connection on Citrix VM_RDP SSH

symptom: Black screen after RDP login to Azure VM with Citrix XenApp/XenDesktop 7.15 LTSR CU2 or 7.17 VDA with Profile Management enabled; CTRL+ALT+DEL prompt visible but session is black
rootCause: Citrix Profile Management deletes some registry keys after session logoff causing black screen after VDA restart
solution: Update Citrix XenApp/XenDesktop to patched version. Ref: Citrix CTX235100. As workaround, disable Citrix Profile Management temporarily.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureIaaSVM/AzureIaaSVM:/SME Topics/Cant RDP SSH/TSGs/VM Responding/Fail RDP Connection on Citrix VM_RDP SSH

symptom: When attempting to upgrade to SCVMM 2016 from SCVMM 2012 R2, you are presented with a dialog that informs you the database version is not compatible with upgrade.
rootCause: During upgrade we check the Database version in the tbl_GlobalSetting table. The version should be the RTM version of the product being upgraded. If not, you will be presented with an error that the upgrade cannot continue due to an invalid database version.
solution: To upgrade from 2012R2 to 2016, you can modify the setting in the database with the following query: Update [dbo].[tbl_VMM_GlobalSetting]SET PropertyValue = '3.2.7510.0' WHERE PropertyName = 'DatabaseVersion'
sourceRef: ContentIdea#59294

symptom: IMPORTANT - GetSub is in process of deprecation, Azure Support Center-> Resource explorer is the new tool. if you have questions please see the Deprecation FAQ below. Get-Sub.ps1 is a PowerShell script that automates ACIS, Fabric, MDS and COSMOS operations and visualizes the output in an HTML report. Get-Sub reports are automatically generated at case submissions. A link to the report is in the Communications section of the MSSolve case in a mail from Azure Diagnostics Automation. You can run Get-Sub manually from Azure Support Center or from PowerShell on your local machine. Get-Sub only needs subscription ID to run. There are additional switches that can be used to control its behavior. View Get-Sub parameters by running show-command gs (shows them in a GUI), or by using PowerShell's tab completion by typing gs -<TAB> (gs, then a space, then a dash, then hit <TAB> to cycle through the parameters). Sample output for a subscription: https://azuresupportcenter.msftcloudes.com/getsubrepo...

symptom: SCVMM configures whether to use Winrm over http or https during the installation of the VMM Server. In case, the VMM was installed to communicate through winrm over http, however the command below was run on the hyper-v node (to enable the winrm listener on https), the VMM server would not be able to contact VMM agent on this Hyper-v server/node:winrm quickconfig -transport:httpsYou would start seeing the errors similar to following for it while refreshing the node/clusterError (2927)A Hardware Management error has occurred trying to contact server wdc1tvhyprv03.nciwin.local .WinRM: URL: [http://wdc1tvhyprv03.nciwin.local:5985], Verb: [ENUMERATE], Resource: [http://schemas.microsoft.com/wbem/wsman/1/wmi/root/cimv2/Win32_Service], Filter: [select * from Win32_Service where Name=WinRM]Unknown error (0x80338170)Recommended ActionCheck that WinRM is installed and running on server wdc1tvhyprv03.nciwin.local. For more information use the command winrm helpmsg hresult and http://support.micr...
rootCause: Configuration of the port to be used by VMM server to contact the Hyper-v nodes in the same domain can be done during installation of VMM server only.
solution: Run the command below on the hyper-V node to remove the winrm listener on HTTPS:winrm delete winrm/config/Listener?Address=*+Transport=HTTPSAlso, run the following to reset the certificate usage with winrmwinrm set winrm/config/service '@{CertificateThumbprint=}'

symptom: Need to deploy SSIS packages from a client machine to a remote SQL server running in Azure Virtual machine. Trying to do so from Visual Studio on the client returns the following error messageFailed to connect to server <server name> (Microsoft.SqlServer.ConnectionInfo) Timeout expired. The timeout period elapsed prior to obtaining a connection from the pool. This may have occurred because all pooled connections were in use and max pool size was reached. (System.Data)
rootCause: It is not possible to deploy SSIS packages directly to a SQL instance running on Azure VM. You have to use one of the options mentioned in https://blogs.msdn.microsoft.com/ssis/2017/01/05/running-ssis-on-azure-vm-iaas-do-more-with-less-money/
solution: Note:Works for small number of packages:Here are the steps that I tried and verified that they work:1) Open a RDP session to your Azure VM instance hosting your SQL server instance.2) Install SSDT tools from https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/ssdt/download-sql-server-data-tools-ssdt on the remote instance.3) Create SSIS catalog (if not already done) using ôIntegration Services Catalogö menu under Databases.4) On your client machine navigate to the folder where you have your SSIS package (for example: c:\\ssispackages\\) and right click to copy the package you want to deploy.5) On the remote desktop window, in windows explorer, navigate to the folder where you want to save these packages (for example c:\\packagesonvm) and then do a paste.6) Launch SQL Server data tools (SSDT) on the remote desktop window, add the package you copied in (Step 5) to a SSIS project and then deploy to the local SQL instance.

symptom: When attempting to add an S2D (Storage Spaces Direct) or SOFS (Scale Out File Server) Cluster to the Storage Provider Fabric in a Clustered Virtual Machine Manager 2016 system, you receive the following error: Error (26311) Registration of the storage subsystem <CLUSTER> for user <ACCOUNT> failed from <VMM NODE> with error code 1 [SM_RC_NOT_SUPPORTED]. Recommended Action Specify a valid subsystem name, and user credentials for registering the subsystem.
rootCause: This can happen when the VMM Cluster was upgraded from an earlier version of Windows to Window Server 2016. The system managing a storage cluster must be at or above the same Cluster Functional Level.
solution: To resolve the issue, be sure all nodes of the VMM Cluster are up using the following Powershell command: get-clusternode Once you have confirmed all nodes in the Cluster are Up, run the following command: Update-ClusterFunctionalLevel Note that even if the Cluster Functional Level shows as a 9 on both clusters, running the command to update the cluster functional level on the VMM server may still be necessary to solve the issue.

symptom: Scenario Host not responding and/or Host needs attention errors are related to communications issues from the SCVMM Server to the SCVMM Agent on a managed host machine. Scoping Host not responding and/or Host needs attention errors manifest themselves in many different scenarios. For example, a VM deployment may encounter a failure. The root of the problem isn't always related to the VM deployment, the failure may occur if SCVMM is unable to communicate with the Host agent and/or received an unexpected response. Likewise for many other common SCVMM jobs where 'Host not responding' and/or 'Host needs attention' is the culprit of the issue ; VM migration, vNetwork deployment, executing host property changes, VM Refresh, Host refreshàetc.. symptom: Scenario SCVMM management consists of three main areas. symptom: Windows 2012 R2 VMs may experience poor performance if the VM is storage stack is in emulated mode. Local resource VHD (temp disk) I/O performance may also show severe performance degradation.
rootCause: The issue is caused due to a recently discovered bug in Windows which is exposed during VM provisioning phase. During VM provisioning storflt driver is getting uninstalled from the VM storage device stack unexpectedly. This causes the VM storage stack to use the slower path involving emulated mode. The driver remains uninstalled and does not get re-installed on subsequent restarts. Mitigation steps below resolves the issue permanently for the affected VM.
solution: Currently there are two methods to mitigate the issue. Method:1 Using Device Manager Gui inside the VM Open Device manager ( devmgmt.msc) Select Disk drives\\Virtual HD ATA Device, right click and select properties, Check for the presence of 5 drivers are in the Device stack, as shown below. NOTE: The problem occurs if only 4 drivers are seen - and is missing storflt in the list below. For each system\\devices\\Microsoft Hyper-V Storage Accelerator Right click and select properties, then select ôupgrade driverö\\Browse my computer\\Let me pick from a list, then choose ôMicrosoft Hyper-V Storage Acceleratorö Reboot the VM Check again in Disk drives\\Virtual HD ATA Device to ensure all disk have the device stack as in screenshot above. Method 2: The script provided below mitigated the disk performance issue as expected. # (1) PnP Clean Write-Host Running: PnP Clean. RUNDLL32.exe pnpclean.dll,RunDLL_PnpClean /devices /maxclean # (2) Configure the startup type of the storflt service Write-Host Running: Setting boot type of storflt service. $output = sc.exe config storflt start=boot if($output.Contains(SUCCESS) -eq $false) { Write-Host Error while setting the boot type of the service. [$output] Exit } # (3) Read the registry key for LowerFilters Write-Host Running: Reading registry value. $regValue = Get-ItemProperty -Path HKLM:\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Class\\{4d36e967-e325-11ce-bfc1-08002be10318}\\ -Name LowerFilters if($regValue.LowerFilters.Contains(storflt) -eq $false) { Writ...

symptom: The vm deployment failed for specific site. It used to fail at Install VM components stage with below error Error (2912): An internal error has occurred trying to contact the <HOST_NAME> server: NO_PARAM: NO_PARAM Unknown error (0x80072f0d) Recommended Action Ensure the agent is installed and running. Ensure the WS-Management service is installed and running, then restart the agent . In VMM etl trace 706442,05:02:09.668 11-15-2017,0x1694,0x1B2C,4,NewVmSubtaskBase.cs,2124,0x00000000,DeploymentException [ex#ffffffffffffffff] caught by RunNewVmSubtasks (catch CarmineException) [[(DeploymentException#1c3d4e) { Microsoft.VirtualManager.Engine.Deployment.DeploymentException: An internal error has occurred trying to contact the host1.contoso.com server: NO_PARAM: NO_PARAM.WinRM: URL: [http://host1.contoso.com:5985]; Verb: [INVOKE]; Method: [GetError]; Resource: [http://schemas.microsoft.com/wbem/wsman/1/wmi/root/microsoft/bits/BitsClientJob?JobId={125E6508-D5F8-4EEA-BD6F-F41DFFDA738F}] Check ...
rootCause: This can happen if the 443 is bound to other application certificate. In my case Azure site recovery(ASR) application was using 443 and it bound to ASR certificate.
solution: In log we found that deployment is failed with this hex code 0x80072F0D That code translates to Certificate issue We have verified the certificate and found the VMM and host are in sync When we ran Netsh http show sslcert on host Found 443 is bound to ASR application certificate So, customer changed the ASR application port from 443 to 8084 After that VM deployment was successful.

symptom: Whenever Import-SCLibraryPhysicalResource operation is performed on clustered library share or SOFS library share. But same operation works standalone library share. The Job fails with below error Error (24381) The transfer did not succeed. The failure could be due to timeout on the server, client cancelling the job, or client cmdlet failure. Recommended Action Try running the cmdlet again. This vmiso sofs share was added to scvmm as library share and trying to import the iso to this share.
rootCause: Its Bug 119830 . I tested on both VMM 2012 R2 and VMM 2016. For some reason on cluster nodes the application is unable to create the certificate binding. But on stand library as soon as we try to import, its creates certificate binding with 443 as show below.
solution: It will be fixed on VMM 2016 UR5 and CB2. But if its VMM 2012 R2 then try the below workaround. So, on SOFS cluster nodes, I ran below command to manually to create the certificate binding. Login to SOFS cluster node Run->Certlm.msc Locate the SCVMM certificate Double click on it and go to thumbprint Copy the thumbprint and remove the space between them Input your thumbprint on below command and run from elevated command prompt on file server cluster node netsh http add sslcert ipport=0.0.0.0:443 certhash= adad7075a8402d006dd5995348c0bb3419665582 appid={00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000} certstorename=MY urlretrievaltimeout=10000 repeat the step on each sofs cluster node or file server cluster node Now importing the physical resource to highly available library share will succeed.

symptom: When trying to view VM Screenshot in Boot Diagnostics, the following error might happen Failed to get contents of the log 'https://<DIAGStorageAccount.blob.core.windows.net/bootdiagnostics-XYZ/az-XYZ.serialconsole.log?sv=2017-04-17&ss=bqtf&srt=sco&sp=rwdlacup&se=2018-01-26T10:28:58Z&sig=O3%2FbslO3yrmGUq2CWYoXX4hO984zuRD8XcxVTL99jVM%3D' of virtual machine '/subscriptions/<SUBID>/resourceGroups/<RGNAME>/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/<VMNAME>'. Error message: 'description: Failed to complete the RPC call for 'getExtendedInfoBlob' message: Failed to complete the RPC call for 'getExtendedInfoBlob' stack: ###_RPC_Exception_### From RPC: Microsoft_Azure_Compute -> Microsoft_Azure_Storage (getExtendedInfoBlob) (Callstack capturing is not enabled. Use ?trace=diagnostics to enable it.) '
rootCause: There can be multiple causes to this issue: Virtual Machines with Operating System Windows 2008 R2 (or older) don't produce VM Health Report. So it's expected that no SerialConsole.log is produced in the Boot Diagnostics Storage Account. Hence the error Failed to get contents of the log 'https://<DIAGStorageAccount.blob.core.windows.net/bootdiagnostics-XYZ/az-XYZ.serialconsole.log? You can check your operating system version by opening a Command-Line and execute winver. Virtual Machines with an old VM Guest Agent installed on the VM (ie. Older than 2.7.1198.806) are not supposed to produce a VM Health Report. So it's expected that no SerialConsole.log is produced in the Boot Diagnostics Storage Account. Hence the error Failed to get contents of the log 'https://<DIAGStorageAccount.blob.core.windows.net/bootdiagnostics-XYZ/az-XYZ.serialconsole.log? .You can check the version of VM Guest Agent by opening the C:\\WindowsAzure\\logs\\WaAppAgent.log and search for WindowsAzureGuestAgent starti...
solution: Depending on the cause, you can try: Virtual Machines with Operating System Windows 2008 R2 (or older) don't produce VM Health Report. --> There's no solution here, except upgrade your operating system to a more recent version. Virtual Machines with an old VM Guest Agent installed on the VM (ie. Older than 2.7.1198.806) are not supposed to produce a VM Health Report. --> You can update the VM Guest Agent by installing version 2.7.1198.822 from the MSI on https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=394789&clcid=0x409 If you have error The member's SID could not be resolved in C:\\WindowsAzure\\logs\\TransparentInstaller.log, you'll need to check if the associated used wasn't deleted from the Domain and/or remove these unresolved SIDs (eg. S-1-5-21-XYZ-XYZ) from the Local Administrators group.Also check if you don't have a Domain policy locking down the Local Administrators group, or if the built-in Administrator account isn't removed (0x80005004)If you suspect an issue with the serial port on the Host, you can try to Redeploy the VM to make it moved to another Host. Warning, this action triggers a restart of the VM.

symptom: While trying to deploy a virtual machine (VM) using System Center 2016 Virtual Machine Manager (SCVMM) you may encounter an error.VMM could not locate the specified file/folder on the <SERVER NAME> server. This file/folder might be required as part of another object.Ensure that you have specified a valid path parameter, and that all necessary files/folders are present. Try the operation again.ID: 2903The system cannot find the file specified (0x80070002) You may also see the an error reported in the Install VM components (Step 1.6)
rootCause: This issue can be caused when the virtual disk for the VM is mounted during the phase where SCVMM is trying to install the VM components. During this phase SCVMM will mount the VHD, the load the registry from the VM, and then go through the services registry key. During this step, if it encounters a registry key that is a symbolic link to another registry key, it will fail to be able to locate the target of the symbolic link because the location is not accessible when the registry is loaded from an offline system. This results in a File Not Found error message being returned, which caused the deployment to fail.
solution: In our case the customer did not need the registry key that was created as symbolic link so deleting the key was a valid solution for them. The SCVMM product should probably handle this better, but symbolic links in the registry are very, very rare.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#86892

symptom: SCVMM refresher StorageProviderCacheSync job is failing on SCVMM 2016 The following error is logged:System.Reflection.TargetInvocationException: Exception has been thrown by the target of an invocation. ---> Microsoft.Carmine.WSManMIApiWrappers.WSManMIException: VMM could not enumerate instances of class MSFT_StoragePoolToPhysicalDisk on the server ehv-sv-01.externbeheer.local. Failed with error HRESULT 0x800706be The WS-Management service cannot process the request. The WMI service or the WMI provider returned an unknown error: HRESULT 0x800706be Ensure the provider is running, and then try the operation again. ---> Microsoft.Management.Infrastructure.CimException: The WS-Management service cannot process the request. The WMI service or the WMI provider returned an unknown error: HRESULT 0x800706be
rootCause: The WMIPrvSE process with the StorageWMI provider loaded crashes because too much memory is allocated by the provider and the amount of allocated memory exceed the default WMI quota. A high amount of memory is allocated because the query Select * from MSFT_StoragePoolToPhysicalDisk enumerates a large number or storage objects. Note: the exception thrown by the crashing WMIPrvSE process is ô0xc0000374 - A heap has been corruptedö instead of a more meaningful ôOut or memoryö.
solution: Increase the WMI quota ôMemoryPerHostö. The quota ôMemoryAllHostsö will also have to be adjusted accordingly. This blog article https://blogs.technet.microsoft.com/askperf/2008/09/16/memory-and-handle-quotas-in-the-wmi-provider-service/ explains how to change the values.

symptom: Azure systems fail to boot with a 0x1E KMODE_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED bugcheck. The bugcheck is almost immediate after the system is turned on (<1 second). This issue applies to bugchecks where both of the following are true: The first bugcheck parameter is 0xc0000096 and/or an error code of 0xc0000096 (STATUS_PRIVILEGED_INSTRUCTION) in the !analyze -v output . The assembly instruction being executed is either rdmsr or wrmsr. This can also be found in the !analyze -v output of the dump in the FAULTING_IP or TRAP_FRAME fields. This issue can also be identified by running !mex.kic in the dump. Affected systems can have different looking dumps. For example: 0: kd> !diDump Name: memory.dmpComputer Name: Not FoundWindows 10 Kernel Version 14393 MP (4 procs) Free x64Product: WinNtBuilt by: 14393.2189.amd64fre.rs1_release.180329-1711 <-- April 2018 kernelKernel base = 0xfffff801`c3881000 PsLoadedModuleList = 0xfffff801`c3b89140System Uptime: 0 days 0:00:00.203Bugcheck: 1E (FFFFFFFFC0000096, FFFF...
rootCause: This is a Spectre/Meltdown mitigation related issue that is mitigated in current kernel patch versions (July 2018 and newer).
solution: Action plan: Change registry value to allow system to boot so current patch levels can be applied:1. Attached the broken VM OS disk to a recovery VM.2. Using the recovery VM, load the system registry hive of the broken OS using regedit (Highlight HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE, File->Load Hive)3. Under the 'broken' system hive change the registry key 'FeatureSettingsOverride' from 8 to 3: HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Session Manager\\Memory Management\\FeatureSettingOverride4. Conduct a disk swap via CloudShell for the now fixed managed OS disk: az vm update --name <<VM NAME>> --resource-group <<RESOURCE GROUP>> --os-disk <<RESOURCE INSTANCE ID OF THE FIXED DISK>>5. RDP to the fixed VM and run Windows Update to get the latest kernel applied.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#96771

symptom: General guidance: Customer Reported Incidents (CRIs) should only be filed for requests where a backend operation must be performed that the customer cannot perform themselves, or for issues that exceed the collective knowledge of the Support Delivery team (not individual support engineer). Support Engineers are expected to consult with senior technical resources, Subject Matter Experts (SMEs), and Technical Advisors (TAs) when blocked in troubleshooting an incident. In addition a technical advisor should review the CRI prior to submitting to PG/EEE. The CRI should contain all relevant information to be able to understand the request and analyze upon first receipt of the CRI. It should be assumed that the recipient of the CRI (PG/EEE) will not have access to MSSolve. Therefor the CRI should completely articulate the ask, provide all relevant data that has been collected, explain the customer impact, and detail the troubleshooting steps performed. TA guidance: The TA should review the CR...
rootCause: Lack of clarity on the process to file escalations to Cloud Identity through ViewPoint.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#107949

This queue will be monitored by the Azure Linux Escalation Team. Please review the following process when creating a collaboration for the Azure Linux Escalation Team. For quick questions, please feel free to reach out to our team in the Azure Linux Ninjas Teams Channel. The channel is very active and you can usually get your questions answered fairly quickly. Steps to Create Linux ProblemWhen an Azure engineer receives a Linux case, the engineer will work the case as if it was a Windows case. All support teams in Microsoft work on Linux issues.Engineers are requested to review the troubleshooting guides found in the Azure DevOps Wiki.After performing troubleshooting methodology on the issue, you are welcome to send a collaboration to the Azure ...

symptom: Many Linux Azure Market Place images contain an Azure Linux Agent, this is responsible for completing the setup of the VM when you create it, such as setting the hostname, username, password/ssh keys, mounting the ephemeral disk etc. This is known as æprovisioningÆ, in addition, the agent provides support for Azure VM Extensions. For the images documented, the images will be updated to be provisioned an open source project provisioning agent, æcloud-initÆ. cloud-init offers many additional benefits above the Linux Agent, such as: Performance û Using cloud-init with Azure, in some scenarios, you can see improved reduced VM creation times. VM customization û cloud-init allows you to pass down VM configurations to cloud-init via custom-data, such as running scripts, installing packages, adding users etc. Migration - If you are migrating from other clouds, you can bring cloud-init configurations, and modify them where necessary to work with your Azure deployments.
rootCause: When will the updates take place? Please see this page for details on images being updated, and timeline.
solution: What do you need to do? If you deploy these Azure Market Place images, there is nothing you need to do, just take advantage of the benefits after the update. If you are creating custom images that derive from the above images and use the Linux Agent to process custom-data, then you need to plan to check if your custom-data will still process with cloud-init. cloud-init supports multiple input types, for example this could be a bash script, it could be a cloud-init config etc. Please review the cloud-init user-data documentation.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#113026

symptom: cloud-init is an open source project provisioning agent, that can be baked into Linux images, this is responsible for completing the setup of the VM when you create it, such as setting the hostname, username, password/ssh keys, mounting the ephemeral disk etc. The process of completing the setup is known as æprovisioningÆ. In addition to just setting up the minimum required configuration options described above, it can also process VM configurations, in multiple user-data formats, such as cloud-init configurations, scripts etc.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#113029

symptom: Enable the debug logs by following this article https://supportability.visualstudio.com/AzureAD/_wiki/wikis/AzureAD/183983/Azure-AD-Sign-in-for-Azure-Linux-VMs?anchor=auth.log-(debug-level)in the log you will see the below error,vmTextMining sshd[48164]: pam_aad(sshd:auth): Cannot find the cloud instance for location CentralIndiaThis issue is caused because the metadata sends the location name as IndiaCentral and Plugin is searching for CentralIndia.
rootCause: This issue is caused because the metadata sends the location name as IndiaCentral and Plugin is searching for CentralIndia.
solution: This issue is fixed in the Plugin release 1.0.011360001 to install this plugin please follow the below steps,Install the extension with the below command,az vm extension set \\--publisher Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory.LinuxSSH \\--name AADLoginForLinux \\--resource-group <rg name> \\--vm-name <vm name>Login to the VM with a local root account.Create a directory under home directory named plugin using the command mkdir plugin.Navigate to plugin directory using cd plugin.Run the command for Ubuntu version 18.04 curl -O https://packages.microsoft.com/ubuntu/18.04/prod/pool/main/a/aadlogin/aadlogin_1.0.011360001_amd64.deb for Ubuntu version 16.04 use curl -O https://packages.microsoft.com/ubuntu/16.04/prod/pool/main/a/aadlogin/aadlogin_1.0.011360001_amd64.debRun the command apt remove aadlogin.Once the previous command completes run the command dpkg -i aadlogin_1.0.011360001_amd64.debNow if you run the command apt show aadlogin the version will be 1.0.011360001This should update the Plugin set to the recent version and if the install is successful you will get a response as shown in the screenshot below,Restart the sshd service.Now when you try login the devicelogin flow will be triggered.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#114923

symptom: This article will contain a walk through of how to use Dump Insights to scan a dump or review the results of processed data.

symptom: Add virtual machine host job fails with: Add virtual machine host job will fail with: Error (20594)The Windows feature NetworkVirtualization could not be enabled on the computer vmh0.lab.contoso.com because of the following error: Unknown error (0x800f080c) Recommended Action Refer to Windows documentation for how to fix feature installation issues.
rootCause: NetworkVirtualization is required by SCVMM and is not a Feature that ships with Hyper-V Server 2019.

symptom: Purpose of this internal content is to present solution to how to:How to increase quota of a VHDXHow to shrink a VHDX and then specify a new quotaAll these actions require that user has signed out. These actions have to be tested before to present it to customer.With UPD and FSLogix there is currently no supported action plan to either decrease or increase quota on VHDX, or to shrink VHDX. You can use this article to share information with customers, but final decision is up to customer. We have to inform them this is not supported.
rootCause: E.g. after having configured User Profile Disk, it is not possible to change quota of an existing VHDX file from Remote Desktop Service GUI. There is the same limitation with FSLogix, it will not change size (maximum size / quota) for existing disks.
solution: For the 2 actions below the user must have signed out otherwise the VHDX won't be accessible.All the actions below have to be tested to make sure no data will be lost for users.Note: install the Hyper-V-PowerShell moduleGet-WindowsFeature -Name *hyper-v*Install-WindowsFeature -Name Hyper-V -IncludeManagementToolsAction 1: How to increase quota of an existing VHDXMaximum size for this disk is currently .We can also see its MinimumSize has the same value. Remark, currently this disk cannot be shrink below this value.It is possible to check this value from Disk Management console.Let's change maximum size of this VHDX to 30 GBLet's have a look on current status in Disk Management consoleIt is necessary to expend the partition to its new potential maximum valueRemark, the initial VHDX size on disk has not changed.Action 2: How to shrink a disk to its minimal size and then specify a new maximum size for this diskRemark: this second part has to be tested to make sure not data will be lostGoal in this example is to set quota of this disk to 10 GBCurrently it is not possible to use Resize-VHD, the MinimumSize is above the new quota that has to be set.It is possible to resize the disk to its minimal size and then change new size max (quota). This minimal size will depend on the data user added/deleted on the disk.Let's remove fragmentation on disk.It is now necessary to copy/past ACLS of previous disk to the new one, then delete previous disk and rename the new one.Let's resize this d...
sourceRef: ContentIdea#120540

symptom: Error when trying to upgrade/resize Azure VM to a size with more than 64 vCPUs (e.g., Standard_F72s_v2)
rootCause: The guest operating system running on the VM does not support more than 64 vCPUs
solution: Use a supported guest OS that supports >64 vCPUs: Windows Server 2016, Ubuntu 16.04 LTS, SLES 12 SP2, Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.3, CentOS 7.3, or Oracle Linux 7.3 with LIS 4.2.1

symptom: In-place upgrade of Windows client VM (Win10/Win11) on Azure fails or becomes blocked, requiring direct console access
rootCause: In-place system upgrades are only supported for specific Windows client versions (Windows 10 single-session all editions). Unsupported versions include Win10/11 single-to-multi-session, Windows 8.1, and Windows 7. Also the upgrade causes disconnection between data plane and control plane, disabling Auto guest patching, Hotpatching, and Azure Update Manager.
solution: For supported Win10 single-session: use Settings > Windows Update > Check for updates > Download and install Feature Update. For unsupported versions: download VHD, attach to local Hyper-V VM, perform in-place upgrade locally, then upload VHD back to Azure. Ensure VM has ≥2GB RAM and ≥12GB free disk space. Back up VM before upgrade. Run OS Upgrade Assessment Tool first.

symptom: 32-bit Windows OS on Azure VM only shows 1GB of available memory; remaining memory appears as reserved within the guest VM. VM extensions (Azure Backup, Azure Monitor Agent) do not work.
rootCause: Azure platform imposes a memory address space limitation on VMs running 32-bit operating systems, restricting available memory to 1GB (especially on client SKUs like Windows 7/10). Linux and Windows VM Guest Agent does not support 32-bit OS, so extensions depending on it fail.
solution: Migrate to 64-bit operating system version. Note: only Specialized VHDs are supported for 32-bit OS on Azure (Generalized images won't work). Some 32-bit OS versions have reached end of life and won't receive further support.

symptom: Windows Update fails on Azure VM with servicing stack corruption errors such as 0x80073712 (ERROR_SXS_COMPONENT_STORE_CORRUPT), 0x800F0831 (CBS_E_STORE_CORRUPTION), 0x80073701 (ERROR_SXS_ASSEMBLY_MISSING), 0x800F081F (CBS_E_SOURCE_MISSING), 0x800F0830 (CBS_E_IMAGE_UNSERVICEABLE) and other CBS error codes
rootCause: Internal corruption in the Windows servicing stack responsible for managing updates and system components. Missing files, invalid configuration, or corrupted metadata prevent Windows from applying updates or servicing the OS correctly.
solution: Perform in-place upgrade (IPU) of Windows Server within the Azure VM: 1) Back up VM using Azure Backup or snapshot. 2) Review CBS logs at C:\Windows\Logs\CBS for known error codes. 3) Mount installation ISO. 4) Run 'setup.exe /auto upgrade /dynamicupdate disable'. 5) Select 'Keep personal files and apps'. 6) Post-upgrade: verify functionality, reapply custom configs, re-enable Azure Backup/monitoring agents. Alternatively, first try the Windows Update Reset Tool to reset servicing stack (stops wuauserv/cryptsvc/BITS, renames SoftwareDistribution and catroot2 folders, re-registers DLLs).

symptom: Error "Hyper-V cannot be installed because virtualization support is not enabled in the BIOS" when trying to install Hyper-V role on Azure VM with Trusted Launch security type for nested virtualization troubleshooting
rootCause: Trusted Launch VMs do not support nested virtualization. The hypervisor is not enabled in the BCDEdit configuration when security type is set to Trusted launch virtual machines.
solution: 1) Change VM security type to Standard (not Trusted Launch). 2) If hypervisor not enabled, run: bcdedit /set hypervisorlaunchtype auto && Restart-Computer -Force. 3) Use Dv3/Dv4 series VM that supports nested virtualization.

symptom: Azure VM OS disk encrypted with ADE (Azure Disk Encryption) appears locked with a lock icon when attached to repair VM, cannot access volume for offline troubleshooting
rootCause: Azure Disk Encryption (ADE/BitLocker) is enabled on the OS disk. The disk remains locked and inaccessible when attached to a different VM until unlocked with the original BitLocker encryption key (BEK) stored in Azure Key Vault.
solution: For ADE v2 (single-pass) managed disk: use az vm repair create --unlock-encrypted-vm (automated). For semi-automated: attach encrypted disk during repair VM creation (not after), find BEK volume in Disk Management, assign drive letter, run dir H: /a:h /b /s to find .BEK file, then manage-bde -unlock G: -RecoveryKey H:\<BEK-GUID>.BEK. For ADE v1 (dual-pass) or unmanaged: manually retrieve BEK from Key Vault via PowerShell Get-AzKeyVaultSecret.

symptom: Windows VM fails to boot due to corrupted registry hive (SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, etc.) — serial console shows registry error or VM hangs at boot
rootCause: Registry hive file was not properly closed or became corrupted, preventing Windows from loading the configuration needed for boot.
solution: Offline repair: 1) Delete VM (keep OS disk). 2) Attach OS disk to repair VM. 3) Navigate to \Windows\System32\config, backup all files. 4) Open regedit, select HKEY_USERS, File > Load Hive, load the SYSTEM file. 5) If it loads successfully, the hive was just not properly closed — unload it to fix. 6) If Cannot load hive error appears, contact Azure Support. 7) Detach disk and recreate VM.

symptom: Generation 1 Windows VM fails to boot because the Windows partition is not marked as Active in disk partition table
rootCause: The boot partition on the OS disk is not flagged as Active. Generation 1 VMs (BIOS/MBR) require the boot partition to be set as Active to load the OS. Generation 2 VMs (UEFI) do not use active partitions.
solution: Offline repair: 1) Attach OS disk to repair VM. 2) Open diskpart. 3) Select the attached disk and the boot partition (~300-500MB). 4) Run detail partition to check if Active. 5) If not active, run active command. 6) Also run: bcdboot <Windows-partition>:\Windows /S <windows-partition>: to recreate boot record. 7) Detach and recreate VM.

symptom: HealthResources table in Azure Resource Graph has missing entries — VM availability status or health annotations are absent for some VMs
rootCause: The health data emitted by the monitoring service sometimes lacks the associated Resource ID in the event metadata. Without the Resource ID, the payload does not meet Resource Graph data emission specification and is not registered.
solution: Workaround: Use the VM Resource Health blade or Metrics blade in the Azure portal to check availability directly instead of relying on the HealthResources table. Microsoft is implementing architectural changes to reliably attach Resource IDs (Project Flash).

symptom: VM availability metric continues to show Available (value=1) during VM restart — Azure Monitor Metrics, Resource Graph, and Resource Health all report VM as Available even though it is restarting
rootCause: A 15-second delay in the monitoring service gathering health status, plus up to 3 minutes delay in external emission. If VM restart completes within this ~3min15s window, the health change is never externally reported.
solution: Wait at least 3 minutes and 15 seconds after observing Available status before trusting it. Known limitation being addressed by Project Flash infrastructure improvements.

symptom: Azure VM is stuck in Failed state in the Azure portal — VM status shows Failed and cannot be changed
rootCause: The last operation run on the VM failed after its input was accepted, leaving the VM in a failed provisioning state.
solution: Run the Reapply command: Portal: VM > Support + troubleshooting > Redeploy + reapply > Reapply. CLI: az vm reapply -g <RG> -n <VM>. PowerShell: Set-AzVM -Reapply then Update-AzVM. REST: POST .../virtualMachines/{vm}/reapply?api-version=2021-07-01. Requires API version 2019-07-01+.

symptom: Receive alert VM is unavailable for 15 minutes in Azure Resource Health or Activity Log after VM stop/delete/deallocate/restart operation
rootCause: Platform sends unavailability notification when VM is deleted, stopped, deallocated, or restarted. After operation completes, platform may continue reporting poor health for >15 minutes due to health coordination delays.
solution: This is expected behavior in most cases. Check if the operation was user-initiated via Activity Log. The 15-minute unavailability report is a known platform behavior being improved via Project Flash. Use Resource Health for real-time status.

symptom: RDP connection fails with error: The remote session was disconnected because there are no Remote Desktop License Servers available to provide a license. 120-day RD licensing grace period expired.
rootCause: The Remote Desktop Server role licensing grace period (120 days) has expired and no RD License Server is configured.
solution: Workaround: Connect using mstsc file.rdp /admin to bypass licensing for that connection. If only 2 concurrent RDP sessions needed, remove the Remote Desktop Server role via Server Manager.

symptom: RDP connection fails with error: Remote Desktop can't find the computer. DNS resolution failure or incorrect RDP file configuration.
rootCause: The Remote Desktop client cannot resolve the VM's DNS name. Common when using locally stored RDP files with stale DNS names or when proxy blocks HTTPS traffic.
solution: Use the RDP file generated from Azure portal (contains correct FQDN and endpoint port). If on corporate intranet, verify proxy allows HTTPS traffic. Try connecting directly with the VM's public IP.

symptom: RDP authentication error: The Local Security Authority cannot be contacted. User provides credentials in SecurityAuthority\UserName format but authentication fails.
rootCause: VM cannot locate the security authority specified in username. The domain controller may be unavailable (common in Azure VNets), or the computer/domain name is misspelled.
solution: For local accounts use ComputerName\UserName format. For domain accounts verify domain name spelling. If domain controller is unreachable (not started), use a local admin account as workaround.

symptom: RDP error: Your credentials did not work. Occurs after promoting VM to domain controller or when using wrong credential format.
rootCause: After promoting VM to DC in a new AD forest, the local admin account is deleted and replaced with domain account (DOMAIN\Admin). Old local credentials no longer work.
solution: For local accounts use ComputerName\UserName. For domain accounts use DomainName\UserName. After DC promotion, use the new domain credentials (e.g., CORP\DCAdmin) with the same password.

symptom: RDP error: This computer can't connect to the remote computer. User account does not have Remote Desktop sign-in rights.
rootCause: The account used to RDP is not a member of the Remote Desktop Users local group or the Administrators group on the target VM.
solution: Add the user to the Remote Desktop Users local group via MMC snap-in. As workaround, use a domain or local admin account.

symptom: VMAccess extension (enablevmAccess) fails with error: VMAccess Extension does not support Domain Controller. Cannot reset RDP or password on DC VM.
rootCause: The VMAccessAgent extension explicitly does not support VMs configured as domain controllers.
solution: Do not use VMAccess extension on domain controllers. For password reset on DC, use offline method: attach OS disk to repair VM and manually reset via Group Policy scripts.

symptom: VMAccess extension fails with error: The password does not meet the password policy requirements (0x800708C5). Cannot reset password via Set-AzVMAccessExtension.
rootCause: The specified password does not meet Windows password policy requirements (minimum length, complexity, history).
solution: Ensure the new password meets Windows password complexity requirements: minimum 8 chars, uppercase, lowercase, digits, special characters. Check if custom GPO enforces stricter policy.

symptom: Cannot RDP to Azure Windows VM after disabling the default NIC or manually setting a static IP address for the NIC. VM becomes unreachable.
rootCause: Disabling the NIC or setting an incorrect static IP breaks network connectivity. The VM loses its network path and cannot accept RDP connections.
solution: Use Azure portal/PowerShell/CLI to reset NIC: change Private IP to Static with a different available IP in the subnet. Preferred: az vm repair reset-nic. After reconnecting, delete ghost NICs from Device Manager.

symptom: Multiple ghost Mellanox/Hyper-V network adapters appear after Azure VM deallocation (stop/start). Causes connectivity issues, Windows Update failures, Windows Activation failures, stale IP configs. Over 500+ ghosted NICs severely impacts VM.
rootCause: By design with Accelerated Networking: VM moves to new physical host with different hardware MAC address on deallocation. AccelNet bypasses virtual switch so OS cannot reuse previous MAC, creating ghosted NIC devices.
solution: Detect via RunCommand script. Clean up via Device Manager (View > Show hidden devices > remove greyed-out Hyper-V Network Adapters) or RunCommand removal script. Schedule monthly cleanup. No prevention possible with Accelerated Networking.

symptom: RDP connection error: You must change your password before logging on the first time. Cannot log in to Azure VM via RDP because account is flagged for password change on first login.
rootCause: The user account has the 'User must change password at next logon' flag set. RDP does not support interactive password change during initial connection.
solution: If VM agent is running: use Run Command to reset password via 'net user <USERNAME> *'. If VM agent not running: use offline password reset method by attaching OS disk to repair VM.

symptom: RDP fails with CredSSP encryption oracle remediation error: The function requested is not supported.
rootCause: CredSSP update CVE-2018-0886 installed on one side but not the other. Encryption Oracle Remediation policy blocks insecure RDP.
solution: Install CredSSP update on both sides. Workaround: REG ADD AllowEncryptionOracle=2. Use Serial Console or Remote PowerShell.

symptom: Azure Windows VM with multiple IPs loses Internet after configuring secondary IP addresses.
rootCause: Windows selects lowest numerical IP as primary regardless of Azure portal. Only portal-primary IP can reach Internet.
solution: PowerShell: Set-NetIPAddress -IPAddress primaryIP -SkipAsSource false; Set-NetIPAddress -IPAddress secondaryIPs -SkipAsSource true

symptom: Azure VM RDP fails; Netlogon service hung (Event 7022), dependency failure (Event 7001), or terminated with error (Event 7023).
rootCause: Netlogon disabled, hung, or Workstation service dependency failed.
solution: Serial Console: sc query/start Netlogon. If disabled: sc config NETLOGON start=auto. Fix dependencies first.

symptom: Azure VM network connectivity lost; NSI service hung (Event 7022), dependency failure (Event 7001), or account mismatch (Event 7000). DHCP Client cascading failure.
rootCause: NSI service disabled, hung, or account mismatch. Core networking service failure cascades to DHCP and all network services.
solution: Serial Console: sc query/start NSI. If disabled: sc config NSI start=auto. Fix dependencies first.

symptom: Cannot RDP to Azure VM; massive Event 4625 failed logons every second. Session ends unexpectedly. Works via Private IP but not Public IP.
rootCause: Brute force attack on RDP port 3389 consuming service resources.
solution: Enable JIT access. Use Azure Bastion or VPN Gateway. Restrict NSG to specific IPs. Temp change RDP port via Run Command.

symptom: RDP times out or refused due to NSG misconfiguration. Works from one IP but not another.
rootCause: NSG blocking port 3389: no allow rule, priority conflict, subnet/NIC conflict, source IP restriction, wrong service tag.
solution: Use Network Watcher IP Flow Verify. Check effective security rules. Add Allow for TCP 3389. Use Azure Bastion for production.

symptom: RDP fails: No Remote Desktop license server available. Connection stuck at Configuring remote session.
rootCause: RDSH role installed but no valid licensing server. Grace period expired, license not activated, insufficient CALs.
solution: Admin RDP or Serial Console. Set LicensingMode=4. Configure SpecifiedLicenseServers. Remove RDSH if <=2 users needed.

symptom: TermService not starting on Azure VM. Event 7022: Remote Desktop Services hung on starting.
rootCause: TermService stopped/disabled/crashing. Causes: service Disabled, Access Denied, logon failure, crash.
solution: sc query TermService. Disabled -> start= demand; Access Denied -> ProcMon; Logon failure -> reset obj to NetworkService; Crash -> type= own.

symptom: RDP internal error: An internal error has occurred. Connection stuck at Configuring Remote.
rootCause: MachineKeys permission corruption, TLS disabled, RDP certificate corrupted/expired, or VM under attack.
solution: Check NSG. Verify port 3389 used by Termservice. Renew RDP cert. Reset MachineKeys permissions. Enable TLS 1.0/1.1/1.2. Enable NLA.

symptom: RDP login fails with Access is denied. Can connect via mstsc /admin but normal RDP denied.
rootCause: Certificate registry key permission missing for Remote Desktop Users; user profile load failure; Kerberos token too large.
solution: Grant Read on certificate key. Set IgnoreRegUserConfigErrors=1. Set MaxTokenSize=65535. Reset TermService ObjectName.

symptom: RDP disconnects frequently/intermittently. Initial connection succeeds then drops.
rootCause: RDP Listener misconfigured, typically on custom image VMs. Security/encryption/keep-alive/timeout settings incorrect.
solution: Reset RDP listener: SecurityLayer=0, MinEncryptionLevel=1, KeepAliveTimeout=1, fReconnectSame=1, MaxIdleTime=0. Restart VM.

symptom: RDP general error: Remote Desktop cannot connect. Remote access not enabled, computer turned off, or not on network.
rootCause: RDP component disabled: fDenyTSConnections=1, TSEnabled=0, drain mode, listener disabled, logon disabled, or TermService not running.
solution: Serial Console: set fDenyTSConnections=0, TSEnabled=1, TSServerDrainMode=0, fEnableWinStation=1, fLogonDisabled=0. Restart VM.

symptom: RDP fails when Remote Desktop Connection Broker role is installed. Event 2056 (SessionBroker) or Event 1296 (SessionBroker-Client): Logon to the database failed / RD Connection Broker not ready for RPC communication.
rootCause: The hostname of the Remote Desktop Connection Broker server was changed after farm setup. The Windows Internal Database has entries tied to the original hostname, causing broker communication failures.
solution: Reinstall the Remote Desktop Connection Broker role and the Windows Internal Database. Changing hostname after RD farm is built is not supported.

symptom: Cannot RDP to Azure VM. VM boots normally and shows login screen in boot diagnostics, but RDP connection fails with: Remote Desktop can't connect to the remote computer. Static IP configured in guest OS differs from Azure-assigned IP.
rootCause: A static IP address was manually configured on the Windows network interface inside the VM, which differs from the IP assigned by Azure DHCP. This causes network mismatch and RDP unreachability.
solution: Use Serial Console to revert NIC to DHCP: netsh interface ip set address name="<NIC>" source=dhcp. Verify with netsh interface ip show config. No reboot needed. If Serial Console unavailable, use reset-network-interface from Azure portal.

symptom: Cannot RDP or make any connection to Azure VM because the network interface inside the guest OS is disabled. VM appears running but completely unreachable.
rootCause: The network interface (NIC) within the guest Windows OS was disabled, either accidentally or intentionally. All network traffic including RDP is blocked.
solution: Use Serial Console: netsh interface show interface to identify disabled NIC, then netsh interface set interface name="<NIC>" admin=enabled. If Serial Console unavailable, use Azure portal reset-network-interface to change IP (triggers NIC reset).

symptom: Cannot RDP or make any network connection to Azure VM. Boot diagnostics shows VM booted into Safe Mode (Safe Boot minimal or Safe Boot with connectivity). Network interface not available in Safe Mode.
rootCause: VM is configured to boot into Safe Mode (safeboot flag set in BCD). RDP service and most network services do not start in Safe Mode.
solution: Use Serial Console: bcdedit /deletevalue {current} safeboot, then restart VM. If Serial Console unavailable, attach OS disk to recovery VM, run bcdedit /store F: oot cd /deletevalue {Default} safeboot on attached disk, detach and recreate VM.

symptom: Cannot RDP to Azure VM. Guest OS firewall profiles set to block all inbound connections (BlockInboundAlways). Boot diagnostics shows VM fully loaded at Welcome screen but RDP connection refused.
rootCause: Guest OS Windows Firewall inbound policy set to BlockInboundAlways, which ignores all firewall allow rules including RDP. Or RDP firewall rule is specifically disabled.
solution: Via Serial Console: 1) Check profiles: netsh advfirewall show allprofiles. 2) If BlockInboundAlways, set: netsh advfirewall set allprofiles firewallpolicy blockinbound,allowoutbound. 3) Enable RDP rule: netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group="Remote Desktop" new enable=yes. Offline: attach disk to recovery VM, load SYSTEM hive, delete DoNotAllowExceptions registry values from FirewallPolicy profiles.

symptom: Azure VM running Windows Server 2012 R2 experiences poor performance. Severe decrease in local resource VHD (temp disk) I/O performance. VM storage stack is in emulated mode.
rootCause: Bug in Windows exposed when provisioned image has phantom IDE devices. The storflt driver is uninstalled from VM storage device stack unexpectedly, causing storage stack to use slower emulated mode path. Driver remains uninstalled across restarts.
solution: Apply update KB4057903 (Hyper-V integration components update for Windows virtual machines) via Windows Update.

symptom: Azure VM cannot connect to IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254) due to expired or missing intermediate certificates. IMDS-dependent apps fail, WS2022/2025 Azure Edition activation watermark persists, ESU for EOS Windows not installing.
rootCause: Intermediate certificates for IMDS attested data TLS expired or missing from local cert store. Firewall/proxy blocks certificate download URLs.
solution: 1. For WS2022 install KB5036909. 2. Configure firewall/proxy to allow cert downloads per Azure CA details docs. 3. Or download/install all root+subordinate CA certs manually (Local Machine store). 4. Run fclip.exe from elevated cmd. 5. Restart VM.

symptom: Azure VM cannot connect to IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254). IMDS-dependent apps fail, activation watermark displayed. Invoke-RestMethod to IMDS returns error or timeout.
rootCause: Connection to IMDS wire server blocked by web proxy, incorrect routing table (missing/wrong route for 169.254.169.254), or MAC/IP mismatch between Azure NIC and guest OS NIC.
solution: 1. route print - verify 169.254.169.254 route exists. 2. ipconfig /all - match MAC and IP with Azure portal. 3. Fix routing if mismatch. 4. Bypass web proxies for IMDS (-NoProxy in PS6+). 5. Treat 169.254.169.254 same as 168.63.129.16.

symptom: Windows VM shows blue screen error (BSOD) on boot. Boot diagnostics shows stop error: Your PC ran into a problem and needs to restart.
rootCause: Driver problem, corrupted system file or memory, or application accessing forbidden memory sector.
solution: 1. Attach OS disk to recovery VM. 2. Locate Memory.dmp in Windows folder. 3. If no dump: enable via registry (CrashDumpEnabled=1, NMICrashDump=1 on ControlSet001/002), enable serial console (bcdedit /ems). 4. Reattach, reproduce, collect dump. 5. Submit support ticket with dump.

symptom: Windows VM stuck on Getting ready or Getting Windows ready. Dont turn off your computer. VM does not reach login screen after update/config change.
rootCause: Server performing final restart after configuration change (Windows Update or role/feature change). Large updates need extended reconfiguration time.
solution: 1. Restore VM from backup if available. 2. Otherwise attach OS disk to recovery VM, locate Memory.dmp. 3. Enable dump log+serial console via registry if no dump. 4. Reattach, send NMI to trigger dump, collect and submit.

symptom: Boot diagnostics shows: A disk read error occurred. Press Ctrl+Alt+Del to restart. VM cannot boot.
rootCause: Disk structure corrupted and unreadable. For Gen1 VMs, boot partition may not be set to Active.
solution: 1. Create repair VM, attach OS disk. 2. Gen1: diskpart check/set partition active. 3. bcdboot to recreate boot record. 4. chkdsk /f on attached disk. 5. Enable serial console and dump collection. 6. Rebuild VM.

symptom: Azure Windows VM doesn't start, boot diagnostics shows BitLocker recovery key prompt: 'Plug in the USB driver that has the BitLocker key' or 'Enter the recovery key to get going again'
rootCause: VM cannot locate the BitLocker Recovery Key (BEK) file from Azure Key Vault to decrypt the encrypted OS disk
solution: Stop and deallocate the VM, then start it (forces BEK retrieval from Key Vault). If that fails: attach OS disk to recovery VM, disable EncryptionSettingsEnabled, retrieve BEK from Key Vault via PowerShell (Get-AzKeyVaultSecret), save as .BEK file, unlock with manage-bde -unlock F: -RecoveryKey. For KEK scenario, use unwrapkey REST API to decrypt wrapped BEK first.

symptom: Azure Windows VM (domain controller) stuck in reboot loop, boot diagnostics shows stop code 0xC00002E1 (STATUS_DS_CANT_START) or 0xC00002E2 (STATUS_DS_INIT_FAILURE)
rootCause: LSASS.exe cannot authenticate because the domain controller has no read/write access to its local Active Directory database (NTDS.DIT). Causes: disk full, NTDS.DIT missing/corrupt, SAN policy set to offline for non-OS disks, or disk not attached
solution: Attach OS disk to repair VM. 1) Free disk space if <300MB. 2) Verify NTDS.DIT disk is attached and online via registry query. 3) Enable DSRM boot: bcdedit /set {identifier} safeboot dsrepair. 4) Rebuild VM. 5) Fix SAN policy to ONLINEALL via diskpart. 6) Remove safeboot flag after fix.

symptom: Azure Windows VM shows 'Windows could not finish configuring the system. To attempt to resume configuration, restart the computer. Setup is starting services' during boot
rootCause: The OS is unable to complete the Sysprep process. Occurs on initial boot of a generalized VM when the image is in an un-deployable state
solution: The image cannot be recovered. Recreate the generalized image following Azure guidance on preparing/capturing an image. Re-deploy from the new generalized image.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck during Windows Update with error C01A001D: 'Error C01A001D applying update operation'. Boot diagnostics shows WU in progress but failing.
rootCause: OS disk is full — the operating system cannot write core files to disk during Windows Update KB installation
solution: Attach OS disk to repair VM. 1) Resize disk up to 1TB if not at max (Expand-AzDisk). 2) Run Disk Cleanup tool. 3) Defragment drive: defrag <letter>: /u /x /g. Reassemble VM after freeing space.

symptom: Windows VM caught in reboot loop: boot diagnostics shows VM booting but process gets interrupted and starts over repeatedly.
rootCause: Three possible causes: (1) Third-party service flagged as critical cannot start; (2) OS changes from update/app install/new policy causing boot failure; (3) Registry hive corruption.
