solution: Attach OS disk to rescue VM. Cause 1: Check ErrorControl registry value for RDAgent/Azure services under BROKENSYSTEM ControlSet, change from 2 to 1. Cause 2: Boot to Last Known Good Configuration. Cause 3: Copy files from regback to config folder.

symptom: Azure VM startup stuck at Windows Update screen: messages like Installing Windows ##%, We could not complete the updates Undoing changes, Failure configuring Windows updates Reverting changes.
rootCause: Windows Update installation or rollback process stuck during boot. Update packages left in Install Pending or Uninstall Pending state.
solution: Wait 8 hours first. If still stuck: attach OS disk to rescue VM, run dism /image:F:\ /get-packages to find pending packages, then dism /Remove-Package to remove the stuck update.

symptom: Windows installation error during VM boot: The computer restarted unexpectedly or encountered an unexpected error. Windows installation cannot proceed.
rootCause: Custom Unattend.xml answer file used with sysprep generalize. Custom answer files are not supported in Azure.
solution: Re-run sysprep without /unattend flag: sysprep /oobe /generalize /shutdown. Do not use custom Unattend.xml when preparing images for Azure.

symptom: VM stuck at Windows Boot Manager menu: Choose an operating system to start, or press TAB to select a tool. VM does not boot automatically.
rootCause: BCD flag displaybootmenu is enabled, causing Boot Manager to wait for user input which is not possible in Azure.
solution: Via Serial Console: bcdedit /set {bootmgr} timeout 5 or bcdedit /deletevalue {bootmgr} displaybootmenu. Offline via rescue VM: use bcdedit /store with the BCD path.

symptom: Windows Server 2016 VM unresponsive after applying Windows Update (specifically KB5003638). Boot diagnostics shows update failing with error code C01A001D (STATUS_LOG_FULL).
rootCause: Insufficient disk space or permission issue with files in %systemroot%\system32\config\TxR directory after applying KB5003638 on builds below 14393.4350.
solution: Before applying KB5003638: ensure 1GB free space, apply KB5001347 or KB5003197 first, install Known Issue Rollback 062521 01.msi, enable KB5001347 Issue 001 Rollback policy, restart, then apply KB5003638.

symptom: Azure VM unresponsive during boot showing Applying Audit Policy Configuration policy message. Applies to Windows Server 2012/2012 R2.
rootCause: Conflicting locks when Delete user profiles older than N days on system restart policy (CleanupProfiles) attempts to clean old user profiles.
solution: Attach OS disk to rescue VM. Load SOFTWARE hive as BROKENSOFTWARE. Delete CleanupProfiles key via reg delete. Unload hive. Do not use CleanupProfiles policy on VMs.

symptom: Azure VM (domain controller) unresponsive on restart, boot diagnostics shows Default Domain Controllers Policy message.
rootCause: Recent changes to Default Domain Controllers Policy causing boot hang. Specific root cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Undo recent Default Domain Controllers Policy changes. If unknown cause: attach OS disk to rescue VM, collect memory.dmp, submit support ticket with dump file.

symptom: Azure VM unresponsive during boot showing Applying Group Policy Local Users and Groups policy or Please wait. Applies to Windows Server 2012/2012 R2.
rootCause: Conflicting locks when Delete user profiles older than N days policy (CleanupProfiles) attempts to clean old user profiles during Group Policy processing.
solution: Attach OS disk to rescue VM. Load SOFTWARE hive as BROKENSOFTWARE. Delete CleanupProfiles key via reg delete. Use scheduled task instead of CleanupProfiles policy for profile cleanup.

symptom: Azure VM unresponsive during boot, boot diagnostics screenshot shows OS stuck with message: Applying security policy to the system
rootCause: Multiple potential causes requiring memory dump analysis. OS hangs while applying security policy during boot, possibly due to corrupted policy, Group Policy processing issues, or driver conflicts.
solution: 1) Create repair VM using VM Repair Commands (steps 1-3). 2) Attach OS disk to repair VM. 3) Enable Serial Console and memory dump collection via bcdedit and registry. 4) Rebuild VM using step 5 of VM Repair Commands. 5) Collect memory dump from \Windows\MEMORY.DMP and submit to support.

symptom: Azure VM unresponsive during boot, stuck at 'Please wait for the Local Session Manager' message shown in boot diagnostics screenshot
rootCause: Multiple potential causes requiring memory dump analysis. VM is stuck waiting for Local Session Manager service to complete initialization.
solution: 1) Wait up to 1 hour as process may self-resolve. 2) If persists, prepare repair VM using VM Repair Commands (steps 1-3). 3) Connect via RDP. 4) Locate memory.dmp on attached OS disk at \Windows\memory.dmp. 5) If dump not found, use NMI calls via Serial Console to generate crash dump. 6) Submit support ticket with dump file.

symptom: Azure VM fails to boot with Windows Boot Manager error: Status 0xC0000428 - Windows cannot verify the digital signature for this file (winload.exe)
rootCause: The VM was built from a preview/trial image with an expiration date. Once the preview period expires (e.g., 180 days for Windows preview images), the VM can no longer boot. The image is not an RTM (Release to Manufacturing) version.
solution: 1) Preview image expiration is non-recoverable - cannot extend expiration. 2) Deploy a new VM using a non-preview RTM image. 3) Use PowerShell (Get-AzVMImage) or Azure CLI (az vm image list) to verify if current image is a preview. 4) Transfer data from old VM OS disk to new VM.

symptom: Azure VM BSOD with Windows stop code 0x00000074 BAD_SYSTEM_CONFIG_INFO: Your PC ran into a problem and needs to restart
rootCause: SYSTEM registry hive is corrupted. Possible causes: registry hive not closed properly, hive corruption, or missing registry keys/values.
solution: 1) Delete VM (keep OS disk). 2) Attach OS disk as data disk to troubleshooting VM. 3) Navigate to \Windows\System32\config, backup all files. 4) In Registry Editor, load HKEY_USERS > File > Load Hive > load SYSTEM file. 5) If hive loads without error, it was not closed correctly - unload to fix. 6) If error 'Cannot load... Error while loading hive', contact Azure Support. 7) Detach repaired disk and create new VM from it.

symptom: Azure VM (Windows Server 2008) crashes with blue screen: *** Hardware Malfunction - Call your vendor for support *** The system has halted ***
rootCause: Guest OS was not set up correctly and a Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) was sent. A kernel-mode program generated an uncaught exception.
solution: 1) Restart VM via Azure portal. 2) Once booted, run elevated cmd: REG ADD HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CrashControl /v NMICrashDump /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f. 3) Optionally set CrashDumpEnabled and DumpFile. 4) Enable serial console via BCDEDIT /ems. 5) Restart VM. 6) If VM still cannot boot, create repair VM, manually add registry settings offline, then rebuild.

symptom: Azure VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000017 Status No Memory, displayed in Windows Boot Manager or Recovery screen
rootCause: OS disk is full, too fragmented, or OS cannot access the memory/page file. Page file may be configured on a VHD instead of the temp drive (default Azure location).
solution: 1) Create repair VM, attach OS disk. 2) For Gen2 VMs, assign letter to EFI partition via diskpart. 3) Free disk space: expand to 1TB if <300MB free, run Disk Cleanup, defrag. 4) Clean bad memory from BCD: bcdedit /store <drive>:\boot\bcd /enum {badmemory}, then /deletevalue {badmemory} badmemorylist. 5) Check pagefile location in registry - restore to default temp drive if on VHD. 6) Enable Serial Console and memory dump. 7) Rebuild VM.

symptom: Azure VM BSOD with stop error 0xC000021A STATUS_SYSTEM_PROCESS_TERMINATED: Your PC ran into a problem and needs to restart
rootCause: Critical process failed: Winlogon (winlogon.exe) or Client Server Run-Time Subsystem (csrss.exe) stopped. Causes include mismatched system files, failed SP/KB update, incomplete backup restore, or incompatible third-party software.
solution: 1) Try restoring VM from backup. 2) If no backup: create repair VM, attach OS disk as data disk. 3) Locate \Windows\Memory.dmp on attached disk. 4) If dump not found, use NMI calls via Serial Console. 5) Submit support ticket with memory dump file for analysis.

symptom: Azure VM BSOD with stop error 0x0000007E SYSTEM_THREAD_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED: A system thread exception was not handled, VM needs to restart
rootCause: Cannot be determined until memory dump is analyzed. A system thread generated an exception that the error handler did not catch.
solution: 1) Try restoring VM from backup. 2) Create repair VM, attach OS disk. 3) Connect via RDP to repair VM. 4) If OS disk encrypted, decrypt first. 5) Navigate to \Windows on attached disk, locate Memory.dmp. 6) If not found, use NMI calls via Serial Console. 7) Submit support ticket with dump file.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC000000F: The application or operating system couldn't be loaded because a required file is missing. Affects winload.exe, \Boot\BCD, or system drivers.
rootCause: BCD corruption, missing DEVICE/OSDEVICE references, or missing/corrupt system driver binary on the OS disk.
solution: Attach OS disk to troubleshooting VM. For BCD errors: bcdedit /set device/osdevice partition commands to repair BCD store (Gen1: \Boot\BCD, Gen2: \EFI\Microsoft\Boot\BCD). For missing driver: chkdsk /F, then replace corrupt .sys from WinSxS folder.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC00000BA: a critical system driver is missing or contains errors due to corrupt system files.
rootCause: Windows system files corruption from unfinished installation/erasure, bad application deletion, malware, or improper shutdown.
solution: 1) Disable recently installed service via registry (set Start=4). 2) Repair filesystem: DISM /restorehealth with WinRE or full ISO WIM as source, then SFC /scannow offline.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000011: Windows failed to load because a critical system driver is missing or corrupt.
rootCause: File version mismatch between the binary file indicated in the error and the version the system expects.
solution: Attach OS disk to troubleshooting VM. Run chkdsk /F. Replace corrupt .sys: rename to .sys.old, search WinSxS for latest version, copy to System32\Drivers.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC000014C: The operating system could not be loaded because the system registry file is missing or contains errors.
rootCause: System registry hive is corrupted or was not closed correctly.
solution: Attach OS disk to troubleshooting VM. Backup \windows\system32\config files. Copy from \windows\system32\config\regback to \windows\system32\config to restore registry from backup.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000034 on \Boot\BCD: recovery tools needed.
rootCause: BCD corruption preventing the boot partition from locating the \Windows folder.
solution: Use nested virtualization or attach disk. Rebuild BCD: bcdedit /create {bootmgr}, set description/locale/inherit/displayorder/timeout. Recreate VM.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000098: critical system driver missing or corrupt.
rootCause: Binary file is from a different version of Windows than the VM operating system.
solution: Attach OS disk to troubleshooting VM. chkdsk /F. Replace corrupt .sys from WinSxS. If no match, copy from another working VM with same OS version.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with stop error 0xC0000102 STATUS_FILE_CORRUPT.
rootCause: Corrupt file or disk structure has become corrupt and unreadable.
solution: VM repair commands to create repair VM. chkdsk /F. Replace corrupt binary from WinSxS. Enable Serial Console + memory dump (bcdedit /ems, reg add CrashControl). Rebuild VM.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error 0xC0000359: critical system driver missing or corrupt.
rootCause: The binary file is the 32-bit version and needs to be replaced by the 64-bit version.
solution: Attach OS disk to troubleshooting VM. chkdsk /F. Replace with correct 64-bit .sys from WinSxS folder.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with winload.exe error 0xc000000e: The application or operating system couldn't be loaded because a required file is missing or contains errors.
rootCause: Boot Configuration Data (BCD) references a device/partition that does not exist, causing winload.exe to fail loading the OS.
solution: Attach OS disk to rescue VM. Identify boot and Windows partitions. Use bcdedit to fix BCD: set correct device/osdevice partition for bootmgr and Windows Boot Loader, enable integrityservices, set bootstatuspolicy IgnoreAllFailures. Recreate VM from repaired disk.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with winload.exe error 0xc0000605: A component of the operating system has expired.
rootCause: VM was built from a non-RTM preview/trial image that has passed its expiration date. Preview images have designated lifecycle with trial period.
solution: No fix available for expired image. Must redeploy VM using correct RTM image from Azure Marketplace. Check image description in Marketplace for trial period information before deployment.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with ntoskrnl.exe error 0xc0000221: The operating system couldn't be loaded because the kernel is missing or contains errors.
rootCause: File system corruption on the OS disk causing the kernel file (ntoskrnl.exe) to be unreadable.
solution: Attach OS disk to rescue VM. Run DISM /restorehealth using WinRE image or Windows ISO as source. Run SFC /scannow. If SFC cannot fix, boot from Last Known Good Configuration. Recreate VM from repaired disk.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE error or Boot failure: Reboot and Select proper Boot device.
rootCause: Boot Configuration Data (BCD) is corrupted, or the partition containing the Windows installation is marked inactive.
solution: Stop/deallocate and restart VM first. If persists: attach OS disk to rescue VM. Verify boot partition is active using diskpart. Run bcdboot to create boot record. Repair BCD with bcdedit (set device/osdevice, integrityservices, bootstatuspolicy). Steps differ for Gen1 vs Gen2 VMs.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck during boot on Applying Group Policy Environment policy screen, VM unresponsive.
rootCause: Group Policy Environment policy processing hangs during boot. Specific root cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file from the VM. Create Azure support request with the dump file for further analysis.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck during boot on Applying Group Policy Scheduled Tasks policy screen, VM unresponsive.
rootCause: Group Policy Scheduled Tasks policy processing hangs during boot. Specific root cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file from the VM. Create Azure support request with the dump file for further analysis.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck during boot on Applying Group Policy Registry policy screen, VM unresponsive.
rootCause: Group Policy Registry policy processing hangs during boot. Specific root cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file from the VM. Create Azure support request with the dump file for further analysis.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck during boot on Applying Group Policy Services policy screen, VM unresponsive.
rootCause: Group Policy Services policy processing hangs during boot. Specific root cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file from the VM. Create Azure support request with the dump file for further analysis.

symptom: Windows VM stuck during boot at 'Applying Group Policy Power Options policy' message shown in boot diagnostics screenshot.
rootCause: Group Policy processing stall during boot. Specific cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file via rescue VM, then file Azure support request for dump analysis.

symptom: Windows VM stuck during boot at 'Applying Software Installation policy' message shown in boot diagnostics screenshot.
rootCause: Group Policy software installation policy processing stall during boot. Specific cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file via rescue VM, then file Azure support request for dump analysis.

symptom: Windows VM stuck during boot at 'Please wait for the Group Policy Client' message. VM does not progress past this screen.
rootCause: VM is applying many or complex Group Policy settings at startup. Normal processing can take up to 1 hour.
solution: Wait up to 1 hour for GP processing to complete. If still stuck, collect OS memory dump file and file support request.

symptom: Azure Windows VM BSOD with bug check IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL (0x0000000A). VM fails to boot, blue screen shown in boot diagnostics.
rootCause: OS does not support more than 64 vCPUs but VM is configured with higher vCPU count. E.g. Windows Server 2012 R2 max 64 vCPUs. Kernel-mode driver accessing paged memory at invalid address at raised IRQL.
solution: 1) Reduce vCPUs to 64 or fewer. 2) Rebuild VM with OS supporting >64 vCPUs (Windows Server 2016+). 3) If already supported OS and <=64 vCPUs, collect memory dump for further analysis.

symptom: Azure Windows VM BSOD with bug check BAD_POOL_HEADER (0x00000019). VM crashes with blue screen.
rootCause: Memory pool corruption detected. The pool was already corrupted when the current request was made.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file for detailed dump analysis to determine source of pool corruption. File Azure support request.

symptom: Azure Windows VM BSOD with bug check MEMORY_MANAGEMENT (0x0000001A). VM crashes with blue screen.
rootCause: Server memory management error occurred. Specific cause requires detailed dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file and file Azure support request for dump analysis.

symptom: Azure Windows VM BSOD with bug check KMODE_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED (0x0000001E). VM crashes with blue screen.
rootCause: Kernel-mode program generated an exception that the error handler didn't catch.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file to identify which exception was generated. File Azure support request for dump analysis.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck during boot on 'Applying Group Policy Shortcuts policy' screen. VM unresponsive, cannot reach login screen.
rootCause: Group Policy Shortcuts policy processing hangs during startup. Specific root cause requires memory dump analysis.
solution: Collect OS memory dump file using repair VM, then open support request with the dump attached for analysis.

symptom: Azure Windows VM fails to boot with error: 'An operating system wasn't found. Try disconnecting any drivers that don't contain an operating system.'
rootCause: Startup process cannot locate active system partition — causes include: (1) system partition not marked as active, (2) disk corruption, (3) disk not presented to Hyper-V host, (4) host cannot access storage.
solution: 1. Stop/deallocate and restart VM. 2. If persists: attach OS disk to repair VM → verify partition is active via diskpart → run bcdboot + bcdedit to repair BCD → swap disk back.

symptom: Azure Windows VM stuck on 'Checking file system on C:' or 'Scanning and repairing drive (C:)' during boot. VM does not progress past chkdsk screen.
rootCause: NTFS file system error found after unexpected VM restart or abrupt shutdown. Windows automatically runs chkdsk at next boot.
solution: If VM is stuck and not progressing: 1. Snapshot OS disk as backup. 2. Attach OS disk to repair VM. 3. Run 'chkdsk E: /f' on the attached disk. 4. Detach and reattach as OS disk.

symptom: Azure VM start or resize fails with allocation failure error. Stopped VM cannot be restarted, or existing VM cannot be resized to requested size.
rootCause: Original cluster hosting the VM does not have free space or does not support the requested VM size. Allocation pinned to existing cluster.
solution: For start failure: stop all VMs in the availability set → restart each VM (releases cluster pinning). For resize failure: retry with smaller size, or stop all VMs in availability set → resize → restart. Alternatively, retry at a later time.

symptom: Azure VM stop/start/restart/redeploy fails with error: 'An unexpected error occurred while processing the network profile of the VM. Please retry later.' VM enters Failed state.
rootCause: Problem allocating or updating network resources during VM lifecycle operation.
solution: Reapply the VM state: navigate to VM → Help → Redeploy + reapply → select Reapply. This reruns VM provisioning and resolves the failed state.

symptom: Azure Windows VM shutdown is stuck on Restarting, Shutting Down, or Stopping services screen. Boot diagnostics screenshot shows these messages and the VM does not complete reboot.
rootCause: Windows shutdown process is performing system maintenance (updates, role/feature changes). The process takes a long time depending on number of updates and VM size. Interrupting can corrupt the OS.
solution: 1. Use Serial Console to identify stuck service with Get-Service | Where-Object Status -eq STOP_PENDING. 2. Get PID via tasklist /svc. 3. Collect memory dump with procdump. 4. Kill unresponsive process with taskkill /PID /t /f. 5. After OS boots, run dism /online /cleanup-image /restorehealth if corruption reported.

symptom: Azure VM Start or Redeploy operations take much longer than expected. Guest OS is active but Azure portal shows operation still running. VM extensions are in Failed state.
rootCause: VM extensions have 90-minute provisioning timeout. Failed extensions are re-provisioned during Start/Redeploy, blocking operation completion until success or timeout.
solution: 1. Check extension status in portal VM blade. 2. Check Guest Agent status. 3. Review extension logs in guest OS. 4. Fix or uninstall failed extension. 5. Stop and restart VM.

symptom: Azure VM with many attached VHDs in same storage account reboots unexpectedly. Storage metrics show spikes exceeding scalability targets.
rootCause: Large number of VHDs in single storage account causes IOPS/throughput to exceed scalability targets, resulting in throttling and forced VM reboot.
solution: Distribute VHDs across multiple storage accounts. Max 40 disks per account recommended. Monitor PercentThrottlingError metrics.

symptom: Cannot extend encrypted OS volume (C:) on Azure Windows VM. Extend Volume grayed out in Disk Management. System Reserved partition blocks extension into unallocated space.
rootCause: Custom image has Windows on partition 1 without System Reserved. ADE/BitLocker adds System Reserved as partition 2, blocking OS volume extension.
solution: 1. Stop VM, snapshot OS disk. 2. Resize to larger SKU. 3. Extend System Reserved (leave 200MB). 4. Create new boot volume. 5. bcdboot + copy BCD. 6. Update bcdedit. 7. Mark new partition Active. 8. Delete old System Reserved, extend C:.

symptom: Cannot manually extend/configure volume on SQL Server VM deployed from Azure Marketplace. Storage pool with DATA and LOG volumes already created during deployment.
rootCause: SQL Server Marketplace VMs pre-create a storage pool with DATA and LOG volumes. Manual Disk Management extend is blocked by the pool configuration.
solution: Use Azure portal > VM > SQL Server configuration > Manage SQL virtual machine > Storage Configuration > Configure drive. Extend Data/Log/TempDB volumes from there. Adds disks to storage pool automatically.

symptom: Data disk disappears from Windows guest OS. Event ID 157: Disk has been surprise removed. Disks appear attached in Azure portal but removed in guest.
rootCause: I/O throttling causes disk timeout. Excessive workload + low IoTimeoutValue (default 60s). Low memory or cluster validation can also trigger.
solution: Increase IoTimeoutValue (max 179s). Cluster: run PerfInsights, check cluster logs, upgrade from Standard HDD. Non-cluster: check Azure Monitor for throttling, detach/reattach disk, run chkdsk/sfc.

symptom: Cannot delete Azure storage account/container/blob. Error: storage account cannot be deleted due to artifacts in use, or lease on container/blob.
rootCause: VHD page blobs attached to VMs have active leases. Azure prevents deletion of disks attached to VMs.
solution: Identify blobs via Blob Metadata (MicrosoftAzureCompute_VMName). Delete VM if OS disk. Detach data disks. Retry deletion. For unattached leased blobs: Break Lease first.

symptom: VM deployment fails with InvalidVhd error: VHD footer cookie is not conectix.
rootCause: VHD does not comply with 1MB alignment (size must be 1MB*N), has unallocated spaces, or is corrupted.
solution: Windows: Resize-VHD (Hyper-V role required), convert to fixed-size VHD. Linux: qemu-img. Rebuild VHD if corrupted.

symptom: Cannot enable Azure Disk Encryption. Error: extension version 2.2 is not supported. StatusCode 409 Conflict.
rootCause: Set-AzVMDiskEncryptionExtension without AAD params uses extension v2.2 not registered for subscription.
solution: Method 1: Use AAD parameters (-AadClientID, -AadClientSecret). Method 2: Register-AzProviderFeature UnifiedDiskEncryptionForVMs, wait 10min, Register-AzResourceProvider.

symptom: Guest Agent stuck in Stopping state. NullReferenceException at GoalStateExecutorBase.WaitForExtensionWorkflowComplete.
rootCause: Guest Agent hangs during shutdown due to extension workflow not completing.
solution: A: Restart RdAgent, wait 5min, kill WindowsAzureGuest.exe. B: Uninstall and reinstall latest agent from GitHub.

symptom: Guest Agent MSI install fails. Error 1722 CA2. Setting RdAgent service path in registry failed.
rootCause: WMI StdRegProv not working. VM agent MSI uses WMI for registry access.
solution: Test: wmic StdRegProv GetDWORDValue. Fix: winmgmt /salvagerepository or /resetrepository. Retry MSI.

symptom: WindowsAzureGuestAgent.exe crashes on startup with ConfigurationErrorsException. Event 1026.
rootCause: machine.config missing/corrupted or has WCF profiling entries (ServiceModelSink.Behavior).
solution: Remove ServiceModelSink entries from machine.config, restart services. Or copy machine.config from working VM.

symptom: Guest Agent PFXImportCertStore failed, null handle, Error Code 86. Extensions not working.
rootCause: SYSTEM account lacks Full Control on Crypto folders.
solution: Grant SYSTEM Full Control on C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Crypto\Keys, \RSA, \SystemKeys. Restart services.

symptom: Guest Agent RdCrypt Initialization failed, Error -2147023143. Cannot get transport certificate.
rootCause: CNG Key Isolation (KeyIso) service not running. RPC endpoint not registered.
solution: portqry -n VMName -e 135. Start KeyIso service, restart WaAppAgent.exe.

symptom: Guest Agent BadImageFormatException (0x8007000B). Agent not responding.
rootCause: Enable64Bit registry set to 0. 64-bit apps treated as 32-bit by third-party modification.
solution: Set Enable64Bit to 1 under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\.NETFramework. Restart VM.

symptom: Multiple 'Windows Azure CRP Certificate Generator' certificates accumulate in VM certificate store after daily stop/start cycles. On Linux, numerous .crt files appear in /var/lib/waagent/. Certificates grow over time causing store bloat.
rootCause: Each VM stop/start cycle on a new host issues a new transport certificate for extensions. Old certificates are not automatically cleaned up by older VM agent versions.
solution: 1) Delete old certificates manually (keep only latest). VM agent re-creates if needed. 2) Update VM agent to latest version which auto-cleans old certificates. Windows: Certificates snap-in → Personal → delete old 'CRP Certificate Generator' certs. Linux: remove old .crt files from /var/lib/waagent/.

symptom: VM extension fails with FailedToDecryptProtectedSettings. Errors include: 'Keyset does not exist', 'Invalid provider type specified', 'Self-signed Certificate Generation failed Error Code: -2146893808', 'The enveloped-data message does not contain the specified recipient'. Extension cannot decrypt protected settings.
rootCause: Transport certificate used to decrypt extension protected settings is missing, expired, or has incorrect ACL permissions on MachineKeys/SystemKeys folder. Platform change around May 2020 altered extension certificate workflow causing secondary issues.
solution: 1) Delete 'Windows Azure CRP Certificate Generator' cert from Certificates snap-in. 2) Trigger new goal state: Update-AzVM or Reapply VM. 3) Retry extension. If ACL issue: icacls grant SYSTEM:(F) and Administrators:(RX) on MachineKeys folder. If still failing: stop/deallocate and start VM.

symptom: VM extension fails with Error Code 51 'Unsupported OS'. Extension cannot run on the current operating system.
rootCause: The VM's operating system is not supported by the specific extension. Windows VM Agent requires Windows Server 2008 SP2 (64-bit) + .NET 4.0 minimum. Linux agent has extension framework limits beyond base agent support.
solution: 1) Check individual extension documentation for OS support matrix. 2) Upgrade OS to supported version. 3) For Windows: ensure .NET Framework 4.0+ installed. 4) For Linux: check extension-specific requirements beyond base agent support.

symptom: Windows activation stops working on Azure VM. Error 0xC004F074: No KMS could be contacted. Previously working activation breaks after October 2022. Activation watermark appears.
rootCause: Azure KMS IP addresses changed in July 2022. azkms.core.windows.net now points to 20.118.99.224 and 40.83.235.53 instead of old IPs. VMs with custom routes or firewall rules pointing to old KMS IPs cannot reach new KMS servers.
solution: 1) Test-NetConnection azkms.core.windows.net -Port 1688, also test 20.118.99.224 and 40.83.235.53 port 1688. 2) If custom routes exist: add new KMS IPs 20.118.99.224 and 40.83.235.53 to route table. 3) If firewall rules exist: add outbound allow for both new IPs on port 1688. KMS endpoints: Global=azkms.core.windows.net:1688, China=kms.core.chinacloudapi.cn:1688, USGov=kms.core.usgovcloudapi.net:1688.

symptom: Windows activation fails on Azure VM in forced tunneling scenario (site-to-site VPN or ExpressRoute). Activation request originates from on-premises IP instead of Azure public IP.
rootCause: Forced tunneling routes all internet-bound traffic (including KMS activation traffic on port 1688) to on-premises network. Azure KMS requires activation requests from Azure public IP addresses.
solution: Create Azure custom routes (UDR) to route KMS server IPs directly to Internet next hop. Add routes for azkms.core.windows.net IPs (20.118.99.224, 40.83.235.53) and kms.core.windows.net (23.102.135.246). For Azure China: 159.27.28.100, 163.228.64.161, 42.159.7.249. Attach route table to VM subnet. Verify with psping azkms.core.windows.net:1688.

symptom: Windows activation fails with duplicate Client Machine ID (CMID) error when using self-hosted KMS server. KMS server Duplicate Client Machine ID Report shows multiple VMs with same CMID, causing KMS client count to fall below activation threshold.
rootCause: Azure Marketplace Windows Server images use Sysprep with SkipRearm=1, so VMs from the same image share the same CMID. Self-hosted KMS counts duplicate CMIDs as a single client, potentially failing to meet minimum activation threshold.
solution: For already deployed VMs: run slmgr /rearm from elevated prompt, restart VM, verify CMID changed with cscript slmgr.vbs /dlv, then re-trigger activation. For custom images: re-sysprep source VM with SkipRearm=0 to clear CMID from image.

symptom: Azure Windows VM activation fails with error 0x800705B4 (timeout). Security-SPP Event ID 8196 logged in Application event log.
rootCause: Network connectivity or DNS resolution problems prevent VM from reaching Azure KMS server (azkms.core.windows.net:1688). Custom DNS server may not resolve KMS FQDN. Firewall or NSG may block port 1688.
solution: Set KMS server: slmgr.vbs /skms azkms.core.windows.net:1688. Verify connectivity: Test-NetConnection azkms.core.windows.net -port 1688. Check Network Watcher next hop is Internet for KMS IPs. If custom DNS cannot resolve, switch to Azure internal DNS. Retry activation: loop slmgr.vbs /ato 12 times with 5s delay.

symptom: Azure Windows VM activation fails with error 0xC004FD01 (Windows is not running on a supported Microsoft Hyper-V virtualization platform) or 0xC004FD02 (Windows is not activated on the host machine).
rootCause: VM is configured to use Automatic Virtual Machine Activation (AVMA), which is not supported in Azure. Typically occurs when VM was migrated from on-premises/other cloud, or AVMA key was manually set after deployment.
solution: Change activation from AVMA to KMS: run slmgr /ipk <KMS-client-setup-key> using appropriate key from Microsoft KMS client activation keys list. Then: slmgr /ckms, slmgr /skms azkms.core.windows.net:1688, slmgr /ato.

symptom: Windows Server 2022/2025 Datacenter Azure Edition shows activation watermark on desktop despite KMS activation reporting success via slmgr.vbs /dlv. Pop-up says VM has been deactivated because not running on Azure.
rootCause: Two possible causes: (1) VM cannot reach Azure IMDS endpoint (169.254.169.254) due to web proxy interference. (2) Intermediate certificates for IMDS attested data TLS have expired or are missing.
solution: Cause 1 (IMDS): Bypass web proxies for 169.254.169.254, verify route table entry, check MAC/IP match between Azure and guest OS. Cause 2 (Certs): For WS2022 install KB5036909, verify firewall allows cert downloads from Azure CA, or manually install root/subordinate certificates. Run fclip.exe from system32 and restart VM.

symptom: Allocation failure when deploying VM from custom/gallery/marketplace image. New VM request fails because target cluster cannot support requested VM size or has no free resources.
rootCause: New VM allocation request is pinned to a cluster that either does not support the requested VM size or lacks available capacity.
solution: Cause 1 (size not supported): Retry with smaller size, or stop all VMs in availability set then create new VM. Cause 2 (no free resources): Retry later or create VM in different availability set in same region and add to same VNet.

symptom: Custom image deployment fails with provisioning timeout (generalized uploaded as specialized) or provisioning failure (specialized uploaded as generalized). VM stuck at OOBE screen.
rootCause: Mismatch between actual OS state (generalized/specialized) and the setting used during upload or capture. Generalized OS uploaded as specialized causes timeout; specialized OS uploaded as generalized causes failure with original credentials.
solution: Upload: Use Add-AzVhd with correct setting matching OS state. Run sysprep before uploading as generalized. Capture: Delete current image, recapture from VHDs with correct generalized/specialized setting.

symptom: Cannot attach data disk to VM. Error: AttachDiskWhileBeingDetached — disk is currently being detached or last detach operation failed.
rootCause: Previous data disk detach operation failed, leaving the disk in a transitional 'being detached' state. Reattach attempts are blocked until the failed detach completes.
solution: PowerShell: Get-AzVM, set $vm.StorageProfile.DataDisks[n].ToBeDetached = $true, then Update-AzVM. REST API: PATCH VM with toBeDetached=true on the failing disk (api-version ≥ 2019-03-01). Alternatively remove the disk entry from the payload entirely.

symptom: Cannot deploy VM or VMSS (Uniform) with Capacity Reservation. Deployment fails or reservation is not consumed.
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: (1) Unsupported constraints — Proximity Placement Group, Update Domains, UltraSSD, Spot VMs, Dedicated Hosts, Availability Sets not supported with capacity reservation. (2) >3 Fault Domains not supported. (3) Reservation not explicitly referenced as VM/VMSS property. (4) Cross-subscription reservation not allowed. (5) Running VM must be deallocated first before associating to reservation.
solution: Remove unsupported constraints (PPG, UltraSSD, Spot, Dedicated Host, Availability Set). Limit to ≤3 FDs. Explicitly add capacityReservationGroup property to VM/VMSS. Use same subscription as reservation. Deallocate running VM before associating.

symptom: Provisioning timeout or failure when deploying VM from custom image — generalized image uploaded/captured as specialized or vice versa, VM stuck at OOBE screen
rootCause: Mismatch between the OS type (generalized vs specialized) and the upload/capture setting. Uploading a generalized image as specialized causes timeout; uploading specialized as generalized causes failure with original credentials.
solution: Upload: Use Add-AzVhd with correct generalized/specialized setting (run sysprep first for generalized). Capture: Delete current image and recapture from VHDs with matching OS type setting.

symptom: VM deployment fails with cluster cannot support the requested VM size — AllocationFailed or OverconstrainedAllocationRequest
rootCause: The hardware cluster backing the availability set or region does not have capacity for the requested VM size.
solution: 1) Retry with a smaller VM size. 2) Stop all VMs in the availability set, then create the VM in the desired size — this allows the platform to select a new cluster. 3) For cluster free resource issues, create VM in a different availability set in the same region.

symptom: Cannot attach data disk to VM — error AttachDiskWhileBeingDetached: disk is currently being detached or last detach operation failed
rootCause: Previous data disk detach operation failed, leaving the disk in a transitional state. Attempting to reattach triggers the error.
solution: Set the toBeDetached flag to true on the failing disk via PowerShell (Update-AzVM) or REST API (PATCH with api-version >= 2019-03-01), then update the VM. Alternatively, remove the failing disk entry from the VM payload entirely.

symptom: VM deployment fails with InvalidVhd error — uploaded VHD not supported, disk expected to have cookie value conectix
rootCause: The VHD does not comply with the 1 MB alignment (offset) requirement. Supported disk size should be 1 MB * N (e.g., 102401 MB). Alternatively, the VHD may be corrupted.
solution: Resize the disk to comply with 1 MB alignment: Windows use Resize-VHD PowerShell cmdlet, Linux use qemu-img. If VHD is corrupted, rebuild from scratch following the official prepare VHD documentation.

symptom: Cannot deploy VM or VMSS with Capacity Reservation — deployment fails due to unsupported constraints or configuration
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: (1) Using unsupported features with capacity reservation (Proximity Placement Group, UltraSSD, Spot VMs, Dedicated Hosts, Availability Sets, >3 FDs). (2) Not explicitly associating reservation as VM/VMSS property. (3) Trying to attach running VM without deallocating first. (4) VM/VMSS in different subscription than reservation.
solution: Verify constraints are supported. Explicitly add capacity reservation as a VM/VMSS property. Deallocate running VM/VMSS before associating with reservation. Ensure VM and reservation are in the same subscription.

symptom: Cannot resize VM to a different size family — VM size family not visible when resizing
rootCause: Running VMs are deployed to physical server clusters with specific hardware. Resizing to a size in a different hardware family requires the VM to move to a different cluster, which may not be possible while the VM is allocated on the current cluster.
solution: For Resource Manager VMs: stop all VMs in the availability set before changing the size. For Classic VMs (deprecated): remove and redeploy the cloud service. When creating availability sets, choose the largest expected VM size for the first deployment.

symptom: Capacity Reservation creation fails with insufficient capacity error
rootCause: Azure does not have capacity for the requested VM size + location + quantity combination
solution: Wait and retry later, or try different VM size/location/quantity. Creation is all-or-nothing.

symptom: Capacity Reservation creation fails - VM size unsupported
rootCause: Not all VM sizes supported with Capacity Reservation
solution: Check supported VM sizes at Capacity Reservation limitations docs. Use a supported VM size.

symptom: Capacity Reservation creation fails with insufficient quota
rootCause: Subscription lacks quota for the requested VM size
solution: Request quota increase via Azure portal or try different VM size/location/quantity.

symptom: Cannot delete Capacity Reservation or Group
rootCause: VMs or VMSS still associated to the reservation. Group requires all member reservations deleted first.
solution: Disassociate all VMs/VMSS, delete individual reservations, then delete the Group.

symptom: Capacity Reservation enters Failed provisioning state
rootCause: System error or quantity increase action caused Failed state
solution: Use Application Change Analysis to find previous sku.capacity value, then modify reservation back to that quantity.

symptom: CSE fails with Change is in conflict 409 error
rootCause: VM has CSE installed with different resource name. Only one CSE instance allowed.
solution: Use same -Name as existing CSE, or remove existing CSE first then reinstall.

symptom: CSE fails with FileUris in both protected and public config sections
rootCause: FileUris specified in both public and protected settings sections
solution: Remove existing CSE, reinstall with fileUris in single config section only.

symptom: VM Inspector 403 DiskInspectForbiddenError
rootCause: Missing Disk Backup Reader role on managed OS disk
solution: Get Owner access, run VM Inspector to consent Disk Backup Reader role assignment.

symptom: VM Inspector 405 ResourceNotSupported for encrypted/unmanaged/ephemeral disk
rootCause: VM Inspector does not support encrypted, unmanaged, or ephemeral OS disks
solution: Use alternative diagnostics: Serial Console, Boot Diagnostics. Convert unmanaged to managed if needed.

symptom: Azure Serial Console: Pressing Enter after connection banner does not show sign-in prompt. No SAC prompt appears on Windows VM.
rootCause: Custom VM, hardened appliance, or incorrect boot config causing Windows to fail connecting to serial port. Also occurs on Windows 10 VMs (only Windows Server has EMS enabled by default).
solution: For older/custom images, enable EMS manually: bcdedit /ems {current} on && bcdedit /emssettings EMSPORT:1 EMSBAUDRATE:115200, then reboot. Or use Azure portal Run Command > EnableEMS.

symptom: Azure Serial Console: Only health information shown when connecting to Windows VM. SAC prompt does not appear.
rootCause: Special Administration Console (SAC) has not been enabled for the Windows image. Common on images created before February 2018.
solution: Enable SAC manually via RDP: bcdedit /ems {current} on && bcdedit /emssettings EMSPORT:1 EMSBAUDRATE:115200, reboot. Or use Azure portal Run Command > EnableEMS.

symptom: Azure Serial Console: Unable to type at SAC prompt when kernel debugging is enabled on Windows VM.
rootCause: Kernel debugging conflicts with Serial Console SAC prompt input.
solution: RDP to VM and run: bcdedit /debug {current} off. If cannot RDP, attach OS disk to another VM and run: bcdedit /store <drive>:\boot\bcd /debug <identifier> off, then swap disk back.

symptom: Azure Serial Console: Pasting into PowerShell in SAC results in extra/third character when original content has repeating characters.
rootCause: Known issue with PSReadLine module running in PowerShell session within SAC.
solution: Run Remove-Module PSReadLine to unload PSReadLine from the current session.

symptom: Azure Serial Console: Forbidden response when accessing VM boot diagnostic storage account. Serial Console fails to connect.
rootCause: Custom boot diagnostics storage account has firewall restrictions enabled. Serial Console service IPs are not whitelisted.
solution: Add Serial Console service IPs as firewall exclusions based on VM geography (use SerialConsole service tag). Or use Serial Console in Azure Portal instead of az serial-console CLI.

symptom: Azure Serial Console: Bad Request (400) error when connecting to VM.
rootCause: Boot diagnostics URI is incorrect, e.g., using http:// instead of https://.
solution: Fix boot diagnostics URI: az vm boot-diagnostics enable --name vmName --resource-group rgName --storage https://<storageAccountUri>.blob.core.windows.net/

symptom: Azure Serial Console: Web socket is closed or could not be opened error.
rootCause: Firewall or network policy blocking outbound access to *.serialconsole.azure.com.
solution: Add *.serialconsole.azure.com to firewall allow list. For US Gov cloud, use *.serialconsole.azure.us.

symptom: Azure Serial Console does not work with storage account using Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 with hierarchical namespaces.
rootCause: Serial Console is incompatible with ADLS Gen2 hierarchical namespace storage accounts for boot diagnostics.
solution: Create a separate boot diagnostics storage account without ADLS Gen2/hierarchical namespaces. This setting can only be set at creation time.

symptom: Azure Serial Console connects but no output displayed and user input unresponsive after live migration on Gen2 VM with Trusted Launch and Secure Boot.
rootCause: Serial Console and logs become unavailable after live migration for Gen2 VMs with Trusted Launch + Secure Boot enabled.
solution: Perform a guest OS restart to restore Serial Console functionality.

symptom: Azure Serial Console error: Another connection is currently in progress to this VM.
rootCause: Another user has Serial Console open, or a stale connection exists.
solution: If no other user has Serial Console open, try disabling and re-enabling boot diagnostics to restore access.

symptom: VM error: OSProvisioningTimedOut - OS provisioning did not finish in allotted time. VM guest agent detected but OS not properly prepared.
rootCause: Image not properly generalized (sysprep for Windows / waagent -deprovision for Linux), or provisioning took too long.
solution: Ensure image is properly prepared/generalized. If guest agent detected but times out, use VHD with CreateOption=Attach instead of FromImage.

symptom: VM error: VMAgentStatusCommunicationError - VM has not reported status for VM agent or extensions.
rootCause: VM agent is not running or VM cannot establish outbound connections to Azure storage.
solution: Verify VM has a running VM agent and can establish outbound connections to Azure storage. Check NSG rules and firewall.

symptom: VM error: StorageAccountTypeNotSupported - Boot diagnostics fails because premium storage account is used.
rootCause: Premium storage account type does not support boot diagnostics.
solution: Use a General purpose storage account for boot diagnostics instead of premium storage.

symptom: VM error: OSProvisioningClientError - SSH host key generation failed during Linux VM provisioning.
rootCause: Linux agent (waagent) not set up properly.
solution: Verify Linux agent is set up properly per https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/extensions/agent-linux/

symptom: Computer name of Azure VM using specialized disk is blank in portal/PowerShell/CLI. OS profile properties (computerName, adminUsername, adminPassword) are read-only.
rootCause: When creating a VM from an existing or specialized disk, the OSProfile settings section is not included in the template (only used for generalized images). Azure portal reads computer name from OSProfile, so the field appears blank.
solution: This is a known Azure platform limitation. The VM name provided during resource creation is still displayed, and the hostname inside the guest OS is unchanged. No fix available — cosmetic issue only.

symptom: Pinging a Clustered Name (Failover Cluster Network Name or client access point IP) from non-owner nodes on Azure IaaS VMs returns 'Request timed out'.
rootCause: Expected behavior: Clustered IP address resources have limited functions in Azure networking and do not respond to ICMP ping. The Cluster Name IP is intended for management only, not general network communication. Client access point IPs are Azure Load Balancer addresses with specific port mappings.
solution: Use TCP connectivity tests instead of ICMP ping. For cluster management, connect locally using period (.) or node name. For file share creation, use the active cluster node. This is by-design behavior in Azure Failover Clustering.

symptom: Boot Diagnostics in Azure portal shows error: 'Failed to get contents of the log' when viewing VM screenshot. No SerialConsole.log in storage account.
rootCause: Multiple causes: (1) Windows Server 2008 R2 or earlier does not produce VM Health Report. (2) Old VM Guest Agent version (earlier than 2.7.1198.806) cannot generate report. (3) Unresolved SIDs in Local Administrators group cause VM Guest Agent health report generation to fail.
solution: (1) Upgrade OS to newer version. (2) Update VM Guest Agent to version 2.7.1198.822+. (3) Remove unresolved SIDs from Local Administrators group. (4) Redeploy VM to different host if serial port issue suspected.

symptom: Ubuntu 18.04 LTS VM no longer receives security/feature/maintenance updates after May 31, 2023 (end of standard support). VM becomes vulnerable.
rootCause: Canonical Ubuntu 18.04 LTS reached end of standard support on May 31, 2023. No more security patches from Canonical for the free tier.
solution: Three options: (1) Upgrade to Ubuntu 20.04 LTS then to 22.04 LTS (no direct 18.04→22.04 path). (2) Upgrade to Ubuntu Pro 18.04 for ESM support until April 2028 (in-place upgrade or Marketplace). (3) Deploy fresh Ubuntu 22.04 LTS VM. Note: in-place major version upgrade breaks control plane connection — Azure capabilities like Auto guest patching, Auto OS image upgrades, Hotpatching, Azure Update Manager become unavailable.

symptom: Cannot connect to Azure Linux VM after disabling the default NIC or manually setting a static IP for the NIC
rootCause: The default network interface (eth0) is disabled or has incorrect static IP configuration, preventing network connectivity
solution: Reset the NIC using Azure portal (change IP to static, assign new available IP, VM restarts), Azure PowerShell (Set-AzNetworkInterfaceIpConfig), or Azure CLI (az vm repair reset-nic). After connectivity restored, optionally revert to original IP.

symptom: CentOS VM: eth0 does not seem to be present / Device eth0 has different MAC address than expected / socket IOError No route to host / DoDhcpWork timeout after changing IP from DHCP to fixed
rootCause: HWADDR directive in /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0 contains old MAC address that doesn't match current NIC after VM resize/move, or IP was changed from DHCP to static incorrectly
solution: Remove the HWADDR directive from /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0

symptom: SUSE VM: No configuration found for eth4 / eth0 No interface found - VM not accessible over network
rootCause: Stale udev rules under /etc/udev/rules.d contain entries for old network interface names (eth0/eth1) that no longer match current hardware
solution: Remove the files that contain entries for eth0 or eth1 under /etc/udev/rules.d

symptom: Ubuntu VM: cloud-init-nonet waiting 120 seconds for a network device / route_info failed / Missing required variable: address for eth0/inet
rootCause: (Error 1) Outdated udev saves previous NIC as eth0, new NIC becomes eth1 due to MAC change after resize/move. (Error 2) /etc/network/interfaces.d/eth0.cfg has static config instead of DHCP
solution: (Error 1) Delete /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules and update udev package. (Error 2) Reset eth0.cfg to 'auto eth0; iface eth0 inet dhcp' and remove cached DHCP files in /var/lib/dhcp

symptom: SSH connection to Linux VM fails with: /var/empty/sshd (RHEL) or /var/lib/empty (SUSE) or /var/run/sshd (Ubuntu) must be owned by root and not group or world-writable
rootCause: The sshd privilege separation directory does not exist, is not owned by root, or has incorrect permissions (group/world-writable)
solution: Create directory if missing, set permissions to 755, set ownership to root:root. For RHEL: mkdir -p /var/empty/sshd && chmod 755 && chown root:root. For SUSE: /var/lib/empty. For Ubuntu: /var/run/sshd (runs in memory, restart also fixes). Can use Serial Console, Run Command, or az vm repair.

symptom: SSH connection to Azure Linux VM fails, serial console shows 'Failed to load SELinux policy' and OS cannot complete boot
rootCause: SELinux misconfiguration in /etc/selinux/config, commonly SELINUXTYPE set to 'disabled' instead of 'targeted'/'minimum'/'mls'
solution: (1) Restart VM, interrupt at GRUB menu, add selinux=0 to kernel line to boot with SELinux off, then fix /etc/selinux/config. (2) Use rescue VM with az vm repair, mount OS disk via chroot, correct the config. SELINUXTYPE should be 'targeted', 'minimum', or 'mls'.

symptom: Cannot connect to specialized Debian 9.1 VM after migrating from VMware to Azure
rootCause: NIC name is 'ens33p0' instead of 'eth0' which is required by Azure Linux network configuration. Debian uses predictable network interface names.
solution: Modify GRUB: add 'net.ifnames=0 biosdevname=0' to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX. Update /etc/network/interfaces: remove ens33 entry, add 'auto eth0' and 'iface inet eth0 dhcp'.

symptom: Cannot SSH to Azure Linux VM, syslog shows: Permissions 0777 for '/etc/ssh/sshKeyName' are too open / This private key will be ignored / key_load_private: bad permissions
rootCause: The /etc/ssh configuration directory or files have incorrect permissions (e.g., 0777), accessible by users other than owner. Usually caused by incorrect chmod command.
solution: Restore correct permissions: chmod -R 644 /etc/ssh; chmod 600 /etc/ssh/ssh_host*key; chmod 600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config; chmod 755 /home/<user>; chmod 700 /home/<user>/.ssh; chmod 600 authorized_keys. Use Run Command, Serial Console, or offline repair with az vm repair.

symptom: Linux VM encounters page allocation failure (GFP_ATOMIC) indicating memory allocation failure under atomic context
rootCause: System runs out of available memory pages; can be caused by unexpected load spikes, memory fragmentation, memory leaks, misconfigured memory parameters, kernel bugs, or lack of swap space
solution: Diagnose with free/vmstat/top/htop/cat /proc/meminfo/buddyinfo. Scale up VM memory, reconfigure cgroup limits, adjust huge page allocation, or configure swap space on ephemeral disk via cloud-init or waagent.conf

symptom: Linux VM logs Out of memory: Kill process or invoked oom-killer - OOM Killer terminates processes
rootCause: Both RAM and swap are exhausted; OOM Killer selects process with highest oom_score/RSS. Causes: memory leaks, too many processes, insufficient VM size
solution: Identify high-memory process via top/ps. Check for memory leaks. Scale up VM size. Configure swap on ephemeral disk. Reconfigure cgroup memory limits

symptom: Linux VM logs Failed to fork: Cannot allocate memory - systemd or applications cannot create new processes
rootCause: Insufficient free memory to allocate initial process state for new fork. System under severe memory pressure
solution: Scale up VM memory, configure swap space, identify and fix memory-consuming processes or leaks, adjust cgroup/overcommit settings

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot with Kernel panic - VFS: Unable to mount root fs on unknown-block(0,0) or missing initramfs image
rootCause: Missing initramfs image after kernel update/patching. Initramfs file not generated or GRUB config has missing initrd entry
solution: Boot previous kernel via Serial Console GRUB menu. Regenerate initramfs: depmod + dracut. Regenerate GRUB config. Or use ALAR scripts: az vm repair repair-button --button-command initrd

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot with Kernel panic - Attempted to kill init! after dracut warning
rootCause: Missing important files/directories, missing system core libraries (libc.so.6), wrong file permissions (chmod 777 on /), missing partitions, or SELinux issues
solution: Attach OS disk to repair VM + chroot. For missing libs: rpm --verify + reinstall packages. For wrong permissions: rpm -a --setperms. For SELinux: add selinux=0 then touch /.autorelabel. For missing partitions: recreate with fdisk/gdisk

symptom: Linux VM kernel panic after kernel upgrade/downgrade - kernel BUG or boot failure with new kernel version
rootCause: Kernel upgrade incompatibility or bug; kernel downgrade left missing modules; kernel module changes caused panic
solution: Boot previous kernel via Serial Console or ALAR scripts (az vm repair run --run-id linux-alar2 --parameters kernel). Change default kernel with grubby. Install latest kernel from vendor. Check modules with lsinitrd/lsmod

symptom: Ubuntu VM apt update/install fails with connection timeout to azure.archive.ubuntu.com when VM is behind an internal standard load balancer
rootCause: Internal standard load balancer does not provide default outbound connectivity.
solution: Add public IP, use external LB, configure NAT gateway, or add SNAT outbound rules.

symptom: apt update fails with 470 status code or The repository is no longer signed error on Ubuntu VM
rootCause: Firewall, virtual appliance, or NSG blocking outbound traffic to Ubuntu repo URLs on ports 80/443.
solution: Allow azure.archive.ubuntu.com and packages.microsoft.com through firewall. Add ports 80/443 to NSG outbound allow rules.

symptom: apt update fails with GPG signature error: NO_PUBKEY for third-party repository
rootCause: Third-party repository added but its GPG public key was not imported.
solution: Import GPG key using curl to /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/. Use gpg --dearmor to convert. If key not found, remove the repo entry.

symptom: apt update fails with Temporary failure resolving azure.archive.ubuntu.com
rootCause: Custom DNS resolver not functioning correctly, or DNS server in different subnet without proper UDR.
solution: Switch to Azure default DNS to test. Verify custom DNS on port 53. If DNS in different subnet, ensure correct UDR.

symptom: dpkg error processing linux-image during kernel install: grub-mkconfig Syntax error EOF in backquote substitution
rootCause: Syntax error in /etc/default/grub (e.g., missing closing quotation mark in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX).
solution: Fix syntax error in /etc/default/grub, then retry kernel package installation.

symptom: apt update fails with does not have a Release file for armhf packages on x86_64 Azure VM
rootCause: /etc/apt/sources.list contains ARM (armhf) architecture repos, invalid for x86_64 VMs.
solution: Remove or comment out armhf repository lines from sources.list or sources.list.d/*.list.

symptom: apt update fails with Unknown error executing apt-key and GPG signature verification errors
rootCause: Permission issues on /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/*.gpg - not readable by _apt user. Often caused by umask 0777.
solution: chmod 644 /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/*.gpg. Verify umask in /etc/login.defs is 0022.

symptom: Gen2 Linux VM fails to boot: boot loader did not load an operating system / boot image not found - UEFI partition missing
rootCause: UEFI (EFI System) boot loader partition missing or deleted from OS disk.
solution: Create repair VM via az vm repair, recreate EFI partition with gdisk (correct sector points per distro), restore via az vm repair restore.

symptom: Gen2 Linux VM fails to boot - UEFI EFI partition corrupted (dirty bit set)
rootCause: UEFI vfat partition corrupted, preventing boot loader from reading EFI System partition.
solution: Create repair VM, run fsck.vfat on EFI partition to remove dirty bit, then restore OS disk.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after entire /boot partition contents deleted
rootCause: All /boot contents (kernel, initramfs, grub) deleted and unrecoverable.
solution: Restore VM from backup using Azure Backup - only recovery option.

symptom: Cloud-init Linux VM takes over 2 minutes to start: ephemeral device did not appear after 120 seconds
rootCause: VM size has no local ephemeral disks (Ev4, Dv5, Dasv5). Cloud-init waits 120s for non-existent disk.
solution: Upgrade cloud-init to 21.2+. Workaround: sed maxwait from 120 to 2 in DataSourceAzure.py.

symptom: Cloud-init intermittently writes SSH Host keys to console as ec2 instead of Azure, causing confusion during troubleshooting
rootCause: Cloud-init SSH module calls write-ssh-key-fingerprints helper which uses legacy program name ec2 from original ec2-init project name
solution: Ignore ec2 logs during troubleshooting; this is cosmetic only and does not affect VM functionality

symptom: Cannot resize MBR partition for data disk larger than 2 TB using fdisk; partition stays at 2 TB maximum
rootCause: MBR partition table has 32-bit sector address limitation (2^32 sectors), limiting partition size to 2 TB for 512-byte sector disks
solution: Convert partition from MBR to GPT using gdisk tool: backup data, unmount filesystem, use gdisk to delete and recreate partition as GPT, run fsck, remount and resize filesystem with resize2fs or xfs_growfs

symptom: Azure Linux CustomScript extension 2.0.5 reruns the previous command unexpectedly after auto-update
rootCause: Bug in Microsoft.Azure.Extensions.CustomScript 2.0.5 extension causes previous command to rerun during auto-update from 2.0.x
solution: Uninstall CustomScript extension to avoid rerun, or wait for auto-update to 2.0.6 which fixes the issue

symptom: Linux VM extensions stop running; extension downgrade error in /var/log/waagent.log: Downgrade not allowed; affects Azure Backup and Site Recovery
rootCause: Bug in Linux VM Agent versions 2.2.21 to 2.2.24 prevents extension processing due to downgrade check failure
solution: Remove all *.manifest.xml files in /var/lib/waagent/ to resolve immediately; alternatively wait for Microsoft auto-fix via RunCommandLinux extension

symptom: GPU disabled on Azure N-Series Ubuntu 16.04 LTS VM after upgrading to 4.4.0-75 kernel; kernel NULL pointer dereference BUG in nvidia module
rootCause: Incompatibility between NVIDIA driver and Ubuntu 4.4.0-75 kernel causing kernel NULL pointer dereference in nvidia module
solution: Upgrade kernel to at least version 4.4.0-77

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after migration to Azure or after disabling Hyper-V drivers. VM stuck at dracut/initramfs shell, kernel panic, disk not found errors, no network connectivity. Serial console shows: Warning: /dev/mapper/rootvg-rootlv does not exist or UUID does not exist, dropping to shell.
rootCause: Hyper-V LIS drivers (hv_vmbus, hv_storvsc, hv_netvsc, hv_utils) missing, disabled, or not included in initramfs. Common after migrating from on-premises VMware/KVM to Azure, or after manually disabling hv_ drivers in /etc/modprobe.d/
solution: Solution 1: Create repair VM, mount OS disk via chroot, find and comment out hv_ disable entries: grep -nr hv_ /etc/modprobe.d/ then edit the file. Solution 2: For missing drivers in initramfs, add hv_vmbus hv_netvsc hv_storvsc to /etc/dracut.conf (RHEL/SLES: add_drivers+="hv_vmbus hv_netvsc hv_storvsc") or /etc/initramfs-tools/modules (Ubuntu), then rebuild initramfs via dracut/update-initramfs.

symptom: SLES 12 to 15 migration fails with error: The requested products HPC Module 12 x86_64 are not activated on the system, or Invalid combination of products registered.
rootCause: HPC became a standalone product in SLES 15. The HPC module is no longer available to systems registered as SLES, preventing zypper migration from finding a valid target.
solution: Remove the HPC module before migration: sudo zypper rm sle-module-hpc-release-POOL sle-module-hpc-release. Alternatively, move sle-module-hpc.prod from /etc/products.d/ to /tmp/ and retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 12 to 15 migration fails: suse-migration-sle15-activation not found in package names. No provider of suse-migration-sle15-activation found.
rootCause: SLES 12 Public Cloud module is not enabled by default.
solution: Enable Public Cloud module: sudo SUSEConnect -p sle-module-public-cloud/12/x86_64. On SAP instances, remove sle-ha-release and sle-ha-release-POOL first. Then cleanup: SUSEConnect --cleanup, rm /etc/zypp/{credentials,services,repos}.d/*, rm /var/cache/zypp/*, rm /var/lib/cloudregister/*, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: Generation 2 Azure VM does not start after SLES 15 SP1 to SP2 upgrade. Serial console shows: error: symbol grub_file_filters not found or error: symbol grub_verify string not found when loading kernel.
rootCause: After Generation 2 VM restart/stop/dealloc, Hyper-V in Azure does not preserve startup entries. The SUSE Linux VM cannot start due to corrupted GRUB entries.
solution: Create rescue VM from OS disk snapshot, set up chroot, reinstall GRUB bootloader: sudo /usr/sbin/shim-install --config-file=/boot/grub2/grub.cfg. Then swap snapshot disk back.

symptom: SLES 15 to SP3 migration fails: The requested products are not activated on the system. Migration was previously interrupted or terminated.
rootCause: Incomplete package updates from an interrupted SLES migration causing version mismatch.
solution: Roll back packages to compatible versions: sudo zypper dup, sudo zypper rollback, then retry sudo zypper migration.

symptom: After SLES migration, VM does not start with latest kernel. Repositories are not defined and registration fails.
rootCause: /etc/credentials.d directory has incorrect permissions or corrupted file content.
solution: Clean up registration: rm /var/cache/cloudregister/, rm /etc/zypp/credentials.d/, chmod 0755 /etc/zypp/credentials.d*, registercloudguest --force-new. Then zypper update and reboot.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5 to 15 SP1 DMS migration fails with: Not ready to read within timeout, skipping multiple repositories (HPC, Legacy, Public Cloud, Containers).
rootCause: regionService directory moved from /var/lib to /usr/lib, but DMS scripting looks for certs only under /var/lib.
solution: Create old directory: mkdir -p /var/lib/regionService/certs. Copy certs: cp -a /usr/lib/regionService/certs/* /var/lib/regionService/certs/. Edit /etc/regionserverclnt.cfg to set certLocation = /var/lib/regionService/certs. Then install latest SLES15-Migration and retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5 to 15 SP1 migration fails with: IsADirectoryError: [Errno 21] Is a directory: /system-root/etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp. suse-migration-prepare.service fails.
rootCause: Unknown folder (temp) exists in /etc/pki/trust/anchors directory causing migration preparation to fail.
solution: Move temp folder: sudo mv /etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp /backuplocation/temp. Install migration package: sudo zypper install suse-migration-sle15-activation. Retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3 to SP4 migration succeeds but SP4 to SP5 fails: No migration available. Products listed as (not available). Shows SP3 modules still registered instead of SP4.
rootCause: Required modules not properly activated/deactivated during intermediate migration, causing module version mismatch.
solution: Before migration: activate modules (web-scripting, public-cloud, containers, live-patching) and deactivate legacy/python2/PackageHub modules. Then SUSEConnect --cleanup, rm credentials/services/repos/cache/cloudregister, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3 to SP4 migration fails: Invalid system credentials, probably because the registered system was deleted in SUSE Customer Center. No repositories defined.
rootCause: Certification module existence causes credential validation failure.
solution: Disable certification module before migration: sudo SUSEConnect -d -p sle-module-certifications/15.3/x86_64. Then retry zypper migration.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3 to SP6 zypper migration fails: Signature verification failed for repomd.xml. Skipping repository SLE-Product-SLES15-SP6-Updates. Migration failed with rollback.
rootCause: In mid-2023 SUSE changed GPG signing key for SLE 15 products to RSA 4096 bit key. Old systems lack the new key.
solution: Import new SUSE GPG key: create repo-signing-key-sle-15.txt with the RSA 4096 public key block, then rpm --import repo-signing-key-sle-15.txt. Verify with rpm -q gpg-pubkey (should show 3fa1d6ce-63c9481c). Then retry zypper migration.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after migration to Azure or after disabling Hyper-V drivers. VM stuck at dracut/initramfs, kernel panic, cannot find root device, no network, GRUB failure. Missing hv_vmbus/hv_storvsc/hv_netvsc.
rootCause: Hyper-V/LIS drivers not installed, disabled in /etc/modprobe.d, or missing from initramfs. Common after VMware/KVM migration.
solution: Fix1(disabled): chroot repair VM, grep -nr hv_ /etc/modprobe.d/, remove blacklist. Fix2(missing): add hv_vmbus hv_netvsc hv_storvsc to /etc/dracut.conf or /etc/initramfs-tools/modules, rebuild initramfs.

symptom: SLES 12->15 migration fails: HPC Module 12 x86_64 not activated or Invalid combination of products registered
rootCause: HPC became standalone product in SLES 15, blocks migration target resolution.
solution: Remove HPC: zypper rm sle-module-hpc-release-POOL sle-module-hpc-release. Or mv /etc/products.d/sle-module-hpc.prod /tmp/

symptom: SLES migration: suse-migration-sle15-activation not found in package names
rootCause: SLES 12 Public Cloud module not enabled by default.
solution: SUSEConnect -p sle-module-public-cloud/12/x86_64. For SAP remove sle-ha-release. Cleanup: SUSEConnect --cleanup, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: SLES Gen2 VM won t start after SP1->SP2 upgrade: grub_file_filters not found or grub_verify string not found
rootCause: After Gen2 VM stop/deallocate, Hyper-V doesn t preserve startup entries, GRUB corrupted.
solution: Chroot rescue VM, reinstall GRUB: /usr/sbin/shim-install --config-file=/boot/grub2/grub.cfg

symptom: SLES 15->SP3 migration fails: products not activated, list of SP1 modules not activated
rootCause: Previous SLES migration interrupted/terminated, incomplete package updates.
solution: zypper dup, zypper rollback, zypper migration to retry.

symptom: After SLES migration, VM won t boot with latest kernel, repositories not defined
rootCause: /etc/credentials.d has incorrect permissions or corrupted content.
solution: rm /var/cache/cloudregister/ /etc/zypp/credentials.d/, chmod 0755 /etc/zypp/credentials.d*, registercloudguest --force-new, zypper update, reboot.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5->15 SP1 migration: Not ready to read within timeout for multiple repos
rootCause: regionService dir moved from /var/lib to /usr/lib, DMS only looks under /var/lib for certs.
solution: mkdir -p /var/lib/regionService/certs, cp certs from /usr/lib, update certLocation in /etc/regionserverclnt.cfg.

symptom: SLES migration: IsADirectoryError Errno 21 /system-root/etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp in suse-migration-prepare
rootCause: Unexpected directory in /etc/pki/trust/anchors breaks migration script.
solution: mv /etc/pki/trust/anchors/temp to backup, install suse-migration-sle15-activation, retry.

symptom: SLES 15 SP3->SP4: Invalid system credentials and No repositories defined
rootCause: Certification module conflicts with migration, causes invalid credentials.
solution: SUSEConnect -d -p sle-module-certifications/15.3/x86_64 then retry.

symptom: SLES SP3->SP4/SP5: No migration available, products not available after incomplete migration
rootCause: Required modules not activated while obsolete modules block path.
solution: Activate web-scripting/public-cloud/containers/live-patching, deactivate legacy/python2/PackageHub, SUSEConnect --cleanup, registercloudguest --force-new.

symptom: SLES 12 SP5->15 SP6: Signature verification failed for repomd.xml after intermediate SP3 migration
rootCause: SUSE changed GPG signing key to RSA 4096 mid-2023, old systems lack new key.
solution: Import new GPG key (3fa1d6ce-63c9481c): rpm --import repo-signing-key-sle-15.txt, verify with rpm -q gpg-pubkey, retry zypper migration.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot due to ext4 filesystem corruption. Serial log shows EXT4-fs error, IO failure, or bad extra_isize. VM enters emergency mode.
rootCause: Disk corruption on ext4 filesystem caused by kernel problems, driver errors, or hardware issues.
solution: Identify corrupted disk via serial log. Use fsck /dev/sdX to repair ext4. Repeat fsck until clean. Offline: attach OS disk to rescue VM. Add nofail to non-critical mounts.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot due to XFS filesystem corruption. Serial log shows XFS Metadata CRC error.
rootCause: XFS disk corruption. Uncommitted journal entries or metadata CRC errors.
solution: xfs_repair -n to check, xfs_repair to fix. Journal error: try mount first. If fails, xfs_repair -L (data loss risk). Offline: rescue VM.

symptom: Linux VM boots into emergency mode due to fstab misconfiguration. Timed out waiting for device, Dependency failed.
rootCause: Incorrect /etc/fstab: device names instead of UUID, wrong UUID, missing nofail, syntax errors.
solution: Serial Console single-user mode, fix fstab (UUID+nofail), mount -a, reboot. ALAR: az vm repair repair-button --button-command fstab.

symptom: Linux VM SSH fails, networking down. hv_netvsc disabled in /etc/modprobe.d/. Interface not found for DHCP.
rootCause: Hyper-V network driver (hv_netvsc) disabled via blacklist in /etc/modprobe.d/.
solution: grep -nr hv_netvsc /etc/modprobe.d/, remove disable entries, dracut -f -v or mkinitramfs, reboot.

symptom: Linux VM networking fails after NIC change. MAC address mismatch, cloud-init apply_network_config false.
rootCause: NIC MAC changed but OS config not updated. cloud-init apply_network_config disabled.
solution: Set apply_network_config: true in cloud-init cfg. Or rm /var/lib/cloud/instance/obj.pkl and reboot.

symptom: Linux VM drops to dracut shell. dracut-initqueue timeout, rootvg-rootlv does not exist.
rootCause: hv_vmbus/hv_storvsc disabled in /etc/modprobe.d/. initramfs cannot access root fs.
solution: Offline: rescue VM, chroot, remove hv driver disable entries, dracut -f -v, swap disk.

symptom: Linux VM 3.10 kernel (RHEL/CentOS 7.0-7.1) panics after host memory-preserving update. SCSI sysfs trace.
rootCause: SCSI locking bug in kernel < 3.10.0-327.10.1 triggered by Hyper-V host disk hot-remove.
solution: Restart VM. Update kernel to 3.10.0-327.10.1+ (RHEL/CentOS/Oracle 7.2).

symptom: Linux VM boot fails or apps break after restart because /dev/sdX device names changed.
rootCause: Linux device paths not persistent across reboots. SCSI async scan reorders devices.
solution: Use persistent naming: UUID in fstab, /dev/disk/azure/scsiN/lunN, or LVM names. Add nofail. Update udev rules.

symptom: Oracle Linux 8 SP2 VM: Failed to start Switch Root after GRUB package update.
rootCause: GRUB update corrupts boot entries. kernelopts missing from /boot/loader/entries/.
solution: Boot rescue kernel, copy kernelopts from grubenv, add to boot entry + earlyprintk=ttyS0, reboot.

symptom: Linux VM does not start correctly after LIS 4.1.3 upgrade on kernel 3.10.0-514.16, depmod warnings about unknown symbols
rootCause: Kernel ABI changes in kernel 3.10.0-514.16.1 cause LIS 4.1.3 driver upgrade to fail
solution: Do not upgrade LIS 4.1.3 on kernel 3.10.0-514.16.1. Install LIS 4.2.0 or later.

symptom: Linux VM shuts down or fails to boot when OS disk /var/log/audit partition is full. auditd HALT config triggers shutdown
rootCause: auditd.conf admin_space_left_action=HALT causes system shutdown when audit log disk is full
solution: Change HALT to SUSPEND in /etc/audit/auditd.conf via rescue VM. Free disk space (10% free). Use ALAR auditd action.

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after OS disk resize. cloud-init Errno 28 No space left on device
rootCause: Cloud-init requires some free space to expand root filesystem. Full disk prevents resize
solution: Clear unneeded data via rescue VM or single user mode, then expand filesystem

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot after VFAT disabled. Failed to mount /boot/efi. ADE VMs cannot mount BEK VOLUME
rootCause: VFAT required for /boot/efi (Gen2) and ADE BEK VOLUME. Hardening tools disable via /etc/modprobe.d/
solution: Remove 'install vfat /bin/true' from /etc/modprobe.d/, regenerate initramfs. Gen1 non-encrypted can comment /boot/efi in fstab

symptom: Linux VM fails to boot due to incorrect HugePages. OOM, udev failure, or Kernel panic deadlocked on memory
rootCause: vm.nr_hugepages in /etc/sysctl.conf set too high, reserving all memory for HugePages
solution: Boot single user mode or rescue VM. Reduce vm.nr_hugepages in /etc/sysctl.conf. Regenerate initramfs. Upgrade VM size if needed.

symptom: Swap file not re-created after Linux VM restart on cloud-init provisioned VMs with waagent
rootCause: Both cloud-init and waagent try to configure swap, causing conflict
solution: Disable swap in waagent (ResourceDisk.EnableSwap=n). Create cloud-init per-boot script for swap.

symptom: Cannot access Gen2 Linux VM after reboot. Password authentication fails.
rootCause: Bug in cloud-init 19.3 and earlier on Gen2 VMs breaks password auth after reboot
solution: Upgrade cloud-init to 19.4+. If locked out, use VM Access extension or portal Password Reset.

symptom: Linux VM deployment fails with OSProvisioningInternalError. VM stuck in Creating state ~40min then fails
rootCause: Missing/misconfigured provisioning agent, incorrect image config, or disabled UDF module
solution: Enable boot diagnostics, check serial log. Ensure UDF not blocked. Verify cloud-init/waagent configured. Check /var/tmp not noexec.

symptom: Allocation failure when deploying custom/gallery/marketplace Linux image. Cluster cannot support requested VM size or has no free resources
rootCause: New VM request pinned to cluster that cannot support the VM size or has no available free space
solution: Retry with smaller VM size, or stop all VMs in availability set first then create new VM in desired size. Or create VM in different availability set in same region.

symptom: Provisioning timeout or failure when deploying custom Linux image. Generalized image uploaded as specialized or vice versa
rootCause: Image type mismatch: generalized image uploaded/captured as specialized causes provisioning timeout; specialized as generalized causes provisioning failure with original credentials retained
solution: Upload/capture with correct setting matching OS state. Run -deprovision before uploading as generalized. Recapture from current VHDs with correct generalized/specialized setting.

symptom: Azure Linux VM maximum memory limited to 64 GB despite larger VM size. free -m shows only 64GB inside VM
rootCause: Bug in Linux kernel prior to 3.10 with MTRR handling on Windows Server 2016 Hyper-V hosts (16TB physical address space). Affects SLES 11 SP4, RHEL 6.x, CentOS 6.x
solution: Add disable_mtrr_trim as kernel boot line option in GRUB config. Or upgrade to newer kernel (SLES 12, RHEL 7.x, CentOS 7.x).

symptom: RHEL Pacemaker cluster node does not join after restart. Quorum error, nodes UNCLEAN (offline). Corosync quorum is not configured error in /var/log/messages
rootCause: Missing or incorrect quorum section in /etc/corosync/corosync.conf (VoteQuorum provider not configured)
solution: Add quorum { provider: corosync_votequorum; two_node: 1 } to corosync.conf. Put cluster in maintenance, sync and reload corosync.

symptom: Pacemaker VIP (IPaddr2) resource fails with [findif] failed error
rootCause: NIC is down or no matching route for VIP IP/netmask in default routing table, so findif() cannot determine which NIC to use
solution: Specify nic=eth0 in IPaddr2 resource config: pcs resource update vip_XXX nic=eth0
